problems.title: Erdos Problem #1
problems.statement_preview: If $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,N\}$ with $|A|=n$ is such that the subset sums $\sum_{a\in S} a$ are distinct for all $S\subseteq A$, then must one have $N\gg 2^n$?
problems.established_preview: Major progress known, original stronger form still open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos called this 'perhaps my first serious problem' (in \cite{Er98} he dates it to 1931). The powers of $2$ show that $2^n$ would be best possible here. The trivial lower bound is $N \gg 2^{n}/n$, since all $2^n$ distinct subset sums must lie in $[0,Nn)$. Erdos and Moser \cite{Er56} proved $ N\geq (\tfrac{1}{4}-o(1))\frac{2^n}{\sqrt{n}}. $ (In \cite{Er85c} Erdos offered USD 1…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #3
problems.statement_preview: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ has $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}=\infty$ then must $A$ contain arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions?
problems.established_preview: Partially resolved at low progression lengths; full conjecture open.
problems.references_preview: This is essentially asking for good bounds on $r_k(N)$, the size of the largest subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ without a non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. For example, a bound like $ r_k(N) \ll_k \frac{N}{(\log N)(\log\log N)^2} $ would be sufficient. Even the case $k=3$ is non-trivial, but was proved by Bloom and Sisask \cite{BlSi20}. Much better bounds for $r_3(N)$ we…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #5
problems.statement_preview: Let $C\geq 0$. Is there an infinite sequence of $n_i$ such that $ \lim_{i\to \infty}\frac{p_{n_i+1}-p_{n_i}}{\log n_i}=C? $
problems.established_preview: Substantial partial structure known; full characteri
problems.references_preview: Let $S$ be the set of limit points of $(p_{n+1}-p_n)/\log n$. This problem asks whether $S=[0,\infty]$. Although this conjecture remains unproven, a lot is known about $S$. Some highlights: {UL} {LI}$\infty\in S$ by Westzynthius' result \cite{We31} on large prime gaps,{/LI} {LI}$0\in S$ by the work of Goldston, Pintz, and Yildirim \cite{GPY09} on small prime gaps,{/LI} {LI}Erd…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #9
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be the set of all odd integers not of the form $p+2^{k}+2^l$ (where $k,l\geq 0$ and $p$ is prime). Is the upper density of $A$ positive?
problems.established_preview: Strong partial lower bounds, density positivity still open.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er77c} Erdos credits Schinzel with proving that there are infinitely many odd integers not of this form, but gives no reference. Crocker \cite{Cr71} has proved there are $\gg\log\log N$ such integers in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Pan \cite{Pa11} improved this to $\gg_\epsilon N^{1-\epsilon}$ for any $\epsilon>0$. Erdos believed this cannot be proved by covering systems, i.e. i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #10
problems.statement_preview: Is there some $k$ such that every integer is the sum of a prime and at most $k$ powers of 2?
problems.references_preview: Erdos described this as 'probably unattackable'. In \cite{ErGr80} Erdos and Graham suggest that no such $k$ exists. Gallagher \cite{Ga75} has shown that for any $\epsilon>0$ there exists $k(\epsilon)$ such that the set of integers which are the sum of a prime and at most $k(\epsilon)$ many powers of 2 has lower density at least $1-\epsilon$. Granville and Soundararajan \cite{G…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #12
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be an infinite set such that there are no distinct $a,b,c\in A$ such that $a\mid (b+c)$ and $b,c>a$. Is there such an $A$ with $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>0? $ Does there exist some absolute constant $c>0$ such that there are always infinitely many $N$ with $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert<N^{1-c}? $ Is it true that $ \sum_{n\in A}\frac…
problems.established_preview: Partially resolved with strong constructions and upper constraints; central growth/reciprocal questions remain open.
problems.references_preview: Asked by Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy \cite{ErSa70}, who proved that $A$ must have density $0$. They also prove that this is essentially best possible, in that given any function $f(x)\to \infty$ as $x\to \infty$ there exists a set $A$ with this property and infinitely many $N$ such that $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert>\frac{N}{f(N)}. $ (Their example is given by all integers i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #14
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$. Let $B\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be the set of integers which are representable in exactly one way as the sum of two elements from $A$. Is it true that for all $\epsilon>0$ and large $N$ $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert \gg_\epsilon N^{1/2-\epsilon}? $ Is it possible that $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert =o(N^{1/2})? $
problems.references_preview: Apparently originally considered by Erdos and Nathanson, although later Erdos attributes this to Erdos, S\'{a}rk"{o}zy, and Szemer\'{e}di (but gives no reference), and claims a construction of an $A$ such that for all $\epsilon>0$ and all large $N$ $ \lvert \{1,\ldots,N\}\backslash B\rvert \ll_\epsilon N^{1/2+\epsilon}, $ and yet there for all $\epsilon>0$ there exist infinite…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #15
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^n\frac{n}{p_n} $ converges, where $p_n$ is the sequence of primes?
problems.established_preview: Numerical behavior is compatible with convergence; unconditional proof remains open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos suggested that a computer could be used to explore this, and did not see any other method to attack this. Tao \cite{Ta23} has proved that this series does converge assuming a strong form of the Hardy-Littlewood prime tuples conjecture. In \cite{Er98} Erdos further conjectures that $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^n \frac{1}{n(p_{n+1}-p_n)} $ converges and $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #17
problems.statement_preview: Are there infinitely many primes $p$ such that every even number $n\leq p-3$ can be written as a difference of primes $n=q_1-q_2$ where $q_1,q_2\leq p$?
problems.established_preview: Strong finite positive evidence, infinitude still unproved.
problems.references_preview: The first prime without this property is $97$. The sequence of such primes is A038133 in the OEIS. These are called cluster primes. Blecksmith, Erdos, and Selfridge \cite{BES99} proved that the number of such primes is $ \ll_A \frac{x}{(\log x)^A} $ for every $A>0$, and Elsholtz \cite{El03} improved this to $ \ll x\exp(-c(\log\log x)^2) $ for every $c<1/8$. This is discussed i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #18
problems.statement_preview: We call $m$ practical if every integer $n<m$ is the sum of distinct divisors of $m$. If $m$ is practical then let $h(m)$ be such that $h(m)$ many divisors always suffice. Are there infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m)<(\log\log m)^{O(1)}$? Is it true that $h(n!)<n^{o(1)}$? Or perhaps even $h(n!)<(\log n)^{O(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: It is easy to see that almost all numbers are not practical. Erdos originally showed that $h(n!)<n$. Vose \cite{Vo85} proved the existence of infinitely many practical $m$ such that $h(m)\ll (\log m)^{1/2}$. The sequence of practical numbers is A005153 in the OEIS. A reward of USD 250 is offered in \cite{Er81h}, apparently (although this is not entirely clear) for a proof or d…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #20
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n,k)$ be minimal such that every family $\mathcal{F}$ of $n$-uniform sets with $\lvert \mathcal{F}\rvert \geq f(n,k)$ contains a $k$-sunflower. Is it true that $ f(n,k) < c_k^n $ for some constant $c_k>0$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Rado \cite{ErRa60} originally proved $f(n,k)\leq (k-1)^nn!$. Kostochka \cite{Ko97} improved this slightly (in particular establishing an upper bound of $o(n!)$, for which Erdos awarded him the consolation prize of \$100), but the bound stood at $n^{(1+o(1))n}$ for a long time until Alweiss, Lovett, Wu, and Zhang \cite{ALWZ20} proved $ f(n,k) < (Ck\log n\log\log n)^n …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #25
problems.statement_preview: Let $n_1<n_2<\cdots$ be an arbitrary sequence of integers, each with an associated residue class $a_i\pmod{n_i}$. Let $A$ be the set of integers $n$ such that for every $i$ either $n<n_i$ or $n ot\equiv a_i\pmod{n_i}$. Must the logarithmic density of $A$ exist?
problems.references_preview: This is a special case of [486].

problems.title: Erdos Problem #28
problems.statement_preview: If $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ is such that $A+A$ contains all but finitely many integers then $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Tur\'{a}n. They also suggest the stronger conjecture that $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)/\log n>0$. Another stronger conjecture would be that the hypothesis $\lvert A\cap [1,N]\rvert \gg N^{1/2}$ for all large $N$ suffices. Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy conjectured the stronger version that if $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots\}$ and $B=\{b_1<b_2<\cdots\}$ with $a_n/b_n\to 1$ a…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #30
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(N)$ be the maximum size of a Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that, for every $\epsilon>0$, $ h(N) = N^{1/2}+O_\epsilon(N^\epsilon)? $
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Tur\'{a}n. It may even be true that $h(N)=N^{1/2}+O(1)$, but Erdos remarks this is perhaps too optimistic. Erdos and Tur\'{a}n \cite{ErTu41} proved an upper bound of $N^{1/2}+O(N^{1/4})$, with an alternative proof by Lindstr"{o}m \cite{Li69}. Both proofs in fact give $ h(N) \leq N^{1/2}+N^{1/4}+1. $ Balogh, F"{u}redi, and Roy \cite{BFR21} improved the bo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #32
problems.statement_preview: Is there a set $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert = o((\log N)^2) $ and such that every large integer can be written as $p+a$ for some prime $p$ and $a\in A$? Can the bound $O(\log N)$ be achieved? Must such an $A$ satisfy $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{\log N}> 1? $
problems.references_preview: Such a set is called an additive complement to the primes. Erdos \cite{Er54} proved that such a set $A$ exists with $\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\ll (\log N)^2$ (improving a previous result of Lorentz \cite{Lo54} who achieved $\ll (\log N)^3$). Wolke \cite{Wo96} has shown that such a bound is almost true, in that we can achieve $\ll (\log N)^{1+o(1)}$ if we only ask for al…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #33
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ be such that every large integer can be written as $n^2+a$ for some $a\in A$ and $n\geq 0$. What is the smallest possible value of $ \limsup \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}? $ Is $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>1? $
problems.references_preview: Such a set $A$ is called an additive complement of the set of squares. Erdos observed that there exist $A$ for which the $\limsup$ is finite and $>1$. Moser \cite{Mo65} proved that, for any such $A$, $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}>1.06. $ The best-known lower bound is $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap\{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}\geq\frac{4}{\pi}\approx…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #36
problems.statement_preview: Find the optimal constant $c>0$ such that the following holds. For all sufficiently large $N$, if $A\sqcup B=\{1,\ldots,2N\}$ is a partition into two equal parts, so that $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert=N$, then there is some $x$ such that the number of solutions to $a-b=x$ with $a\in A$ and $b\in B$ is at least $cN$.
problems.references_preview: The minimum overlap problem. The example (with $N$ even) $A=\{N/2+1,\ldots,3N/2\}$ shows that $c\leq 1/2$ (indeed, Erdos initially conjectured that $c=1/2$). The lower bound of $c\geq 1/4$ is trivial, and Scherk improved this to $1-1/\sqrt{2}=0.29\cdots$. The current records are $ 0.379005 < c < 0.380924, $ the lower bound due to White \cite{Wh22} and the upper bound due to Al…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #38
problems.statement_preview: Ask for a set B that is not an additive basis but still guarantees a uniform positive Schnirelmann-density increment for A union (A+b), with b chosen from B.
problems.established_preview: Background gives density increment when B is an additive basis of finite order k. It also notes existence of essential components that are not additive bases.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er36c} proved that if $B$ is an additive basis of order $k$ then, for any set $A$ of Schnirelmann density $\alpha$, for every $N$ there exists some integer $b\in B$ such that $ \lvert (A\cup (A+b))\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert\geq \left(\alpha+\frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)}{2k}\right)N. $ It seems an interesting question (not one that Erdos appears to have asked directly, alt…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #39
problems.statement_preview: Is there an infinite Sidon set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lvert A\cap \{1\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg_\epsilon N^{1/2-\epsilon} $ for all $\epsilon>0$?
problems.references_preview: The trivial greedy construction achieves $\gg N^{1/3}$. The first improvement on this was achieved by Ajtai, Koml\'{o}s, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{AKS81b}, who found an infinite Sidon set with growth rate $\gg (N\log N)^{1/3}$. The current best bound of $\gg N^{\sqrt{2}-1+o(1)}$ is due to Ruzsa \cite{Ru98}. Erdos \cite{Er73} had offered \$25 for any construction which achieves $…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #40
problems.statement_preview: For what functions $g(N)\to \infty$ is it true that $ \lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert \gg \frac{N^{1/2}}{g(N)} $ implies $\limsup 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty$?
problems.references_preview: This is a stronger form of the Erdos-Tur\'{a}n conjecture [28] (since establishing this for any function $g(N)\to \infty$ would imply a positive solution to [28]).

problems.title: Erdos Problem #41
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that the triple sums $a+b+c$ are all distinct for $a,b,c\in A$ (aside from the trivial coincidences). Is it true that $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/3}}=0? $
problems.references_preview: Erdos proved that if the pairwise sums $a+b$ are all distinct aside from the trivial coincidences then $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}=0. $ This is discussed in problem C11 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}, in which Guy says Erdos offered \$500 for the general problem of whether, for all $h\geq 2$, $ \liminf \frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #42
problems.statement_preview: Let $M\geq 1$ and $N$ be sufficiently large in terms of $M$. Is it true that for every Sidon set $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ there is another Sidon set $B\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ of size $M$ such that $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$?
problems.established_preview: Not proved/disproved. For $M=3$, finite behavior is now rigorously non-monotone ($\text{counterexample} \to \text{all-good block} \to \text{counterexample again}$), so no finite-threshold claim is currently justified.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #43
problems.statement_preview: If $A,B\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ are two Sidon sets such that $(A-A)\cap(B-B)=\{0\}$ then is it true that $ \binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rvert}{2}\leq\binom{f(N)}{2}+O(1), $ where $f(N)$ is the maximum possible size of a Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$? If $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert$ then can this bound be improved to $ \binom{\lvert A\rvert}{2}+\binom{\lvert B\rv…
problems.references_preview: Since it is known that $f(N)\sim \sqrt{N}$ (see [30]) the latter question is equivalent to asking whether, if $\lvert A\rvert=\lvert B\rvert$, $ \lvert A\rvert \leq \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-c+o(1)\right)\sqrt{N} $ for some constant $c>0$. In the comments Tao has given a proof of this upper bound without the $-c$. In the comments Barreto has given a negative answer to the secon…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #44
problems.statement_preview: Let $N\geq 1$ and $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ be a Sidon set. Is it true that, for any $\epsilon>0$, there exist $M$ and $B\subset \{N+1,\ldots,M\}$ (which may depend on $N,A,\epsilon$) such that $A\cup B\subset \{1,\ldots,M\}$ is a Sidon set of size at least $(1-\epsilon)M^{1/2}$?
problems.references_preview: See also [329] and [707] (indeed a positive solution to [707] implies a positive solution to this problem, which in turn implies a positive solution to [329]). This is discussed in problem C9 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #50
problems.statement_preview: Schoenberg proved that for every $c\in [0,1]$ the density of $ \{ n\in \mathbb{N} : \phi(n)<cn\} $ exists. Let this density be denoted by $f(c)$. Is it true that there are no $x$ such that $f'(x)$ exists and is positive?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er95} could prove the distribution function is purely singular. References [Er95] Erdos, Paul, Some of my favourite problems in number theory, combinatorics, and geometry. Resenhas (1995), 165-186.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #51
problems.statement_preview: Is there an infinite set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $a\in A$ there is an integer $n$ such that $\phi(n)=a$, and yet if $n_a$ is the smallest such integer then $n_a/a\to \infty$ as $a\to\infty$?
problems.references_preview: Carmichael has asked whether there is an integer $t$ for which $\phi(n)=t$ has exactly one solution. Erdos has proved that if such a $t$ exists then there must be infinitely many such $t$. See also [694]. This is discussed in problems B36 and B39 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #52
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a finite set of integers. Is it true that for every $\epsilon>0$ $ \max( \lvert A+A\rvert,\lvert AA\rvert)\gg_\epsilon \lvert A\rvert^{2-\epsilon}? $
problems.references_preview: The sum-product problem. Erdos and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{ErSz83} proved a lower bound of $\lvert A\rvert^{1+c}$ for some constant $c>0$, and an upper bound of $ \lvert A\rvert^2 \exp\left(-c\frac{\log\lvert A\rvert}{\log\log \lvert A\rvert}\right) $ for some constant $c>0$. The lower bound has been improved a number of times. The current record is $ \max( \lvert A+A\rvert,\lvert…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #60
problems.statement_preview: Does every graph on $n$ vertices with $>\mathrm{ex}(n;C_4)$ edges contain $\gg n^{1/2}$ many copies of $C_4$?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Simonovits, who could not even prove that at least $2$ copies of $C_4$ are guaranteed. The behaviour of $\mathrm{ex}(n;C_4)$ is the subject of [765]. He, Ma, and Yang \cite{HeMaYa21} have proved this conjecture when $n=q^2+q+1$ for some even integer $q$. References [HeMaYa21] He, J. and Ma, J. and Yang, T., Some extremal results on 4-cycles. Journal of…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #61
problems.statement_preview: For any graph $H$ is there some $c=c(H)>0$ such that every graph $G$ on $n$ vertices that does not contain $H$ as an induced subgraph contains either a complete graph or independent set on $\geq n^c$ vertices?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Hajnal \cite{ErHa89}, who proved that a complete graph or independent set must exist on $ \geq \exp(c_H\sqrt{\log n}) $ many vertices, where $c_H>0$ is some constant. This was improved by Buci\'{c}, Nguyen, Scott, and Seymour \cite{BNSS23} to $ \geq \exp(c_H\sqrt{\log n\log\log n}). $ See also the entry in the graphs problem collection. References [BNS…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #62
problems.statement_preview: If $G_1,G_2$ are two graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ then must there exist a graph $G$ whose chromatic number is $4$ (or even $\aleph_0$) which is a subgraph of both $G_1$ and $G_2$?
problems.established_preview: Open in this batch; no proof/counterexample.
problems.references_preview: Erdos also asked \cite{Er87} about finding a common subgraph $H$ (with chromatic number either $4$ or $\aleph_0$) in any finite collection of graphs with chromatic number $\aleph_1$. Every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ contains all sufficiently large odd cycles (which have chromatic number $3$), see [594]. This was proved by Erdos, Hajnal, and Shelah \cite{EHS74}. Erd…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #65
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a graph with $n$ vertices and $kn$ edges, and $a_1<a_2<\cdots $ be the lengths of cycles in $G$. Is it true that $ \sum\frac{1}{a_i}\gg \log k? $ Is the sum $\sum\frac{1}{a_i}$ minimised when $G$ is a complete bipartite graph?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Hajnal. Gy\'{a}rf\'{a}s, Koml\'{o}s, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{GKS84} have proved that this sum is $\gg \log k$, so that only the second question remains. Liu and Montgomery \cite{LiMo20} have proved the asymptotically sharp lower bound of $\geq (\tfrac{1}{2}-o(1))\log k$. See also the entry in the graphs problem collection. See also [57]. References [GKS8…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #66
problems.statement_preview: Is there $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ such that $ \lim_{n\to \infty}\frac{1_A\ast 1_A(n)}{\log n} $ exists and is $ eq 0$?
problems.references_preview: A suitably constructed random set has this property if we are allowed to ignore an exceptional set of density zero. The challenge is obtaining this with no exceptional set. Erdos believed the answer should be no. Erdos and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy proved that $ \frac{\lvert 1_A\ast 1_A(n)-\log n\rvert}{\sqrt{\log n}}\to 0 $ is impossible. Erdos suggests it may even be true that the $\li…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #68
problems.statement_preview: Is $ \sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{1}{n!-1} $ irrational?
problems.established_preview: Open; no full irrationality proof or rationality counterexample found. New finite exclusion evidence: denominator `<=5e6` is impossible.
problems.references_preview: The decimal expansion is A331373 in the OEIS. Weisenberg has observed that this sum can also be written as $ \sum_{k\geq 1}\sum_{n\geq 2}\frac{1}{(n!)^k}. $ Erdos \cite{Er88c} notes that $\sum \frac{1}{n!+t}$ should be transcendental for every integer $t$. References [Er88c] Erd"{o}s, P., On the irrationality of certain series: problems and results. New advances in transcenden…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #70
problems.statement_preview: Let $\mathfrak{c}$ be the ordinal of the real numbers, $\beta$ be any countable ordinal, and $2\leq n<\omega$. Is it true that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\beta, n)_2^3$?
problems.established_preview: No new theorem or counterexample in this batch.
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Rado proved that $\mathfrak{c}\to (\omega+n,4)_2^3$ for any $2\leq n<\omega$.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #74
problems.statement_preview: Given `f(n)->infinity`, is there an infinite-chromatic graph where every `n`-vertex subgraph can be made bipartite by deleting at most `f(n)` edges?
problems.established_preview: Background gives: - Rodl construction for graphs of chromatic number `aleph_0` when `f(n)=epsilon*n` (any fixed `epsilon>0`). - Hypergraph analogue proved.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{EHS82}. R"{o}dl \cite{Ro82} has proved this for hypergraphs, and also proved there is such a graph (with chromatic number $\aleph_0$) if $f(n)=\epsilon n$ for any fixed constant $\epsilon>0$. It is open even for $f(n)=\sqrt{n}$. Erdos offered \$500 for a proof but only \$250 for a counterexample. This fails (even with $f(n)…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #75
problems.statement_preview: Is there a graph of chromatic number $\aleph_1$ such that for all $\epsilon>0$ if $n$ is sufficiently large and $H$ is a subgraph on $n$ vertices then $H$ contains an independent set of size $>n^{1-\epsilon}$?
problems.established_preview: No constructive example or obstruction obtained in this batch.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{EHS82}. In \cite{Er95d} Erdos suggests this may even be true with an independent set of size $\gg n$. See also [750]. References [EHS82] Erdos, P. and Hajnal, A. and Szemer\'{e}di, E., On almost bipartite large chromatic graphs. Theory and practice of combinatorics (1982), 117-123. [Er95d] Erdos, Paul, On some problems in c…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #77
problems.statement_preview: If $R(k)$ is the Ramsey number for $K_k$, the minimal $n$ such that every $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_k$, then find the value of $ \lim_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}. $
problems.references_preview: Erdos offered \$100 for just a proof of the existence of this constant, without determining its value. He also offered \$1000 for a proof that the limit does not exist, but says 'this is really a joke as [it] certainly exists'. (In \cite{Er88} he raises this prize to \$10000). Erdos proved $ \sqrt{2}\leq \liminf_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}\leq \limsup_{k\to \infty}R(k)^{1/k}\leq 4…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #78
problems.statement_preview: Give a constructive proof that $R(k)>C^k$ for some constant $C>1$.
problems.references_preview: Erdos gave a simple probabilistic proof that $R(k) \gg k2^{k/2}$. Equivalently, this question asks for an explicit construction of a graph on $n$ vertices which does not contain any clique or independent set of size $\geq c\log n$ for some constant $c>0$. In \cite{Er69b} Erdos asks for even a construction whose largest clique or independent set has size $o(n^{1/2})$, which is …

Szemer\'{e}di observed that his regularity lemma implies that $f_c(n)\to \infty$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #81
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a chordal graph on $n$ vertices - that is, $G$ has no induced cycles of length greater than $3$. Can the edges of $G$ be partitioned into $n^2/6+O(n)$ many cliques?
problems.established_preview: No new proof in this batch.

The example of all edges between a complete graph on $n/3$ vertices and an empty graph on $2n/3$ vertices show that $n^2/6+O(n)$ is sometimes necessary. problems.title: Erdos Problem #82
problems.statement_preview: Let $F(n)$ be maximal such that every graph on $n$ vertices contains a regular induced subgraph on at least $F(n)$ vertices. Prove that $F(n)/\log n\to \infty$.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos, Fajtlowicz, and Stanton. It is known that $F(5)=3$ and $F(7)=4$. Ramsey's theorem implies that $F(n)\gg \log n$. Bollob\'{a}s observed that $F(n)\ll n^{1/2+o(1)}$. Alon, Krivelevich, and Sudakov \cite{AKS07} have improved this to $n^{1/2}(\log n)^{O(1)}$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #84
problems.statement_preview: The cycle set of a graph $G$ on $n$ vertices is a set $A\subseteq \{3,\ldots,n\}$ such that there is a cycle in $G$ of length $\ell$ if and only if $\ell \in A$. Let $f(n)$ count the number of possible such $A$. Prove that $f(n)=o(2^n)$. Prove that $f(n)/2^{n/2}\to \infty$.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Faudree, who showed that $2^{n/2}<f(n) \leq 2^{n-2}$. The first problem was solved by Verstra"{e}te \cite{Ve04}, who proved $ f(n)\ll 2^{n-n^{1/10}}. $ This was improved by Nenadov \cite{Ne25} to $ f(n) \ll 2^{n-n^{1/2-o(1)}}. $ One can also ask about the existence and value of $\lim f(n)^{1/n}$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #86
problems.statement_preview: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Is it true that every subgraph of $Q_n$ with $ \geq \left(\frac{1}{2}+o(1)\right)n2^{n-1} $ many edges contains a $C_4$?

Erdos \cite{Er91} showed that $ f(n) \geq \left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{c}{n}\right)n2^{n-1} $ for some constant $c>0$, and wrote it is 'perhaps not hopeless' to determine $f(n)$ exactly. problems.title: Erdos Problem #87
problems.statement_preview: Let $\epsilon >0$. Is it true that, if $k$ is sufficiently large, then $ R(G)>(1-\epsilon)^kR(k) $ for every graph $G$ with chromatic number $\chi(G)=k$? problems.established_preview: No new proof; comparison-to-`R(k)` gap remains open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos originally conjectured that $R(G)\geq R(k)$, which is trivial for $k=3$, but fails already for $k=4$, as Faudree and McKay \cite{FaMc93} showed that $R(W)=17$ for the pentagonal wheel $W$. Since $R(k)\leq 4^k$ this is trivial for $\epsilon\geq 3/4$. Yuval Wigderson points out that $R(G)\gg 2^{k/2}$ for any $G$ with chromatic number $k$ (via a random colouring), which asy…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #89
problems.statement_preview: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine $\gg n/\sqrt{\log n}$ many distinct distances?
problems.references_preview: A $\sqrt{n}\times\sqrt{n}$ integer grid shows that this would be the best possible. Nearly solved by Guth and Katz \cite{GuKa15} who proved that there are always $\gg n/\log n$ many distinct distances. A stronger form (see [604]) may be true: is there a single point which determines $\gg n/\sqrt{\log n}$ distinct distances, or even $\gg n$ many such points, or even that this i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #90
problems.statement_preview: Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contain at most $n^{1+O(1/\log\log n)}$ many pairs which are distance 1 apart?
problems.references_preview: The unit distance problem. In \cite{Er94b} Erdos dates this conjecture to 1946. In \cite{Er82e} he offers \$300 for the upper bound $n^{1+o(1)}$. This would be the best possible, as is shown by a set of lattice points. It is easy to show that there are $O(n^{3/2})$ many such pairs. The best known upper bound is $O(n^{4/3})$, due to Spencer, Szemer\'{e}di, and Trotter \cite{SST…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #91
problems.statement_preview: Let $n$ be a sufficently large integer. Suppose $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ has $\lvert A\rvert=n$ and minimises the number of distinct distances between points in $A$. Prove that there are at least two (and probably many) such $A$ which are non-similar.
problems.references_preview: For $n=3$ the equilateral triangle is the only such set. For $n=4$ the square or two equilateral triangles sharing an edge give two non-similar examples. For $n=5$ the regular pentagon is the unique such set (which has two distinct distances). Erdos mysteriously remarks in \cite{Er90} this was proved by 'a colleague'. (In \cite{Er87b} this is described as 'a colleague from Zag…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #92
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that there exists a set $A$ of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ in which every $x\in A$ has at least $f(n)$ points in $A$ equidistant from $x$. Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(1)}$? Or even $f(n) < n^{O(1/\log\log n)}$?
problems.references_preview: This is a stronger form of the unit distance conjecture (see [90]). The set of lattice points imply $f(n) > n^{c/\log\log n}$ for some constant $c>0$. Erdos offered \$500 for a proof that $f(n) \leq n^{o(1)}$ but only \$100 for a counterexample. This latter prize is downgraded to \$50 in \cite{ErFi97}. It is trivial that $f(n) \ll n^{1/2}$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #96
problems.statement_preview: If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon then there are $O(n)$ many pairs which are distance $1$ apart.
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Moser. In \cite{Er92e} Erdos credits the conjecture that the true upper bound is $2n$ to himself and Fishburn. F"{u}redi \cite{Fu90} proved an upper bound of $O(n\log n)$. A short proof of this bound was given by Brass and Pach \cite{BrPa01}. problems.title: Erdos Problem #98
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(n)$ be such that any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with no three on a line and no four on a circle, determine at least $h(n)$ distinct distances. Does $h(n)/n\to \infty$?
problems.established_preview: Attempted framework reduction only; no proof or disproof.
problems.references_preview: Erdos could not even prove $h(n)\geq n$. Pach has shown $h(n)<n^{\log_23}$. Erdos, F"{u}redi, and Pach \cite{EFPR93} have improved this to $ h(n) < n\exp(c\sqrt{\log n}) $ for some constant $c>0$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #99
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points with minimum distance equal to 1, chosen to minimise the diameter of $A$. If $n$ is sufficiently large then must there be three points in $A$ which form an equilateral triangle of size 1?
problems.references_preview: Thue proved that the minimal such diameter is achieved (asymptotically) by the points in a triangular lattice intersected with a circle. In general Erdos believed such a set must have very large intersection with the triangular lattice (perhaps as many as $(1-o(1))n$). Erdos \cite{Er94b} wrote 'I could not prove it but felt that it should not be hard. To my great surprise both…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #100
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that all pairwise distances are at least $1$ and if two distinct distances differ then they differ by at least $1$. Is the diameter of $A$ $\gg n$?
problems.references_preview: Perhaps the diameter is even $\geq n-1$ for sufficiently large $n$. Piepmeyer has an example of $9$ such points with diameter $<5$. Kanold proved the diameter is $\geq n^{3/4}$. The bounds on the distinct distance problem [89] proved by Guth and Katz \cite{GuKa15} imply a lower bound of $\gg n/\log n$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #101
problems.statement_preview: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, no five of which are on a line, the number of lines containing four points is $o(n^2)$.
problems.references_preview: There are examples of sets of $n$ points with $\sim n^2/6$ many collinear triples and no four points on a line. Such constructions are given by Burr, Gr"{u}nbaum, and Sloane \cite{BGS74} and F"{u}redi and Pal\'{a}sti \cite{FuPa84}. Gr"{u}nbaum \cite{Gr76} constructed an example with $\gg n^{3/2}$ such lines. Erdos speculated this may be the correct order of magnitude. This is …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #102
problems.statement_preview: Let $c>0$ and $h_c(n)$ be such that for any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that there are $\geq cn^2$ lines each containing more than three points, there must be some line containing $h_c(n)$ many points. problems.established_preview: No new bound proved in this batch.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #103
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(n)$ count the number of incongruent sets of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ which minimise the diameter subject to the constraint that $d(x,y)\geq 1$ for all points $x eq y$. Is it true that $h(n)\to \infty$?
problems.established_preview: No proof or counterexample in this batch.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #104
problems.statement_preview: Given $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ the number of distinct unit circles containing at least three points is $o(n^2)$.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er81d} Erdos proved that $\gg n$ many circles is possible, and that there cannot be more than $O(n^2)$ many circles. The argument is very simple: every pair of points determines at most $2$ unit circles, and the claimed bound follows from double counting. Erdos claims in a number of places this produces the upper bound $n(n-1)$, but Harborth and Mengerson \cite{HaMe86…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #108
problems.statement_preview: For `r>=4, k>=2`, does there exist finite `f(k,r)` such that every graph with chromatic number at least `f(k,r)` contains a subgraph of girth at least `r` and chromatic number at least `k`?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Hajnal. R"{o}dl \cite{Ro77} has proved the $r=4$ case (see [923]). problems.title: Erdos Problem #111
problems.statement_preview: For `G` with chromatic number `aleph_1`, must `h_G(n)/n -> infinity`, where `h_G(n)` is the deletion cost to make every `n`-vertex subgraph bipartite?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #112
problems.statement_preview: Let $k=k(n,m)$ be minimal such that any directed graph on $k$ vertices must contain either an independent set of size $n$ or a transitive tournament of size $m$. Determine $k(n,m)$.
problems.established_preview: No new sharpened bound in this batch.
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Rado \cite{ErRa67}, who showed $k(n,m) \ll_m n^{m-1}$, or more precisely, $ k(n,m) \leq \frac{2^{m-1}(n-1)^m+n-2}{2n-3}. $ Larson and Mitchell \cite{LaMi97} improved the dependence on $m$, establishing in particular that $k(n,3)\leq n^{2}$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #114
problems.statement_preview: If $p(z)\in\mathbb{C}[z]$ is a monic polynomial of degree $n$ then is the length of the curve $\{ z\in \mathbb{C} : \lvert p(z)\rvert=1\}$ maximised when $p(z)=z^n-1$?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #122
problems.statement_preview: For which number theoretic functions $f$ is it true that, for any $F(n)$ such that $f(n)/F(n)\to 0$ for almost all $n$, there are infinitely many $x$ such that $ \frac{\#\{ n\in \mathbb{N} : n+f(n)\in (x,x+F(x))\}}{F(x)}\to \infty? $
problems.established_preview: No new proof/counterexample beyond known function-specific cases.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #125

problems.title: Erdos Problem #130
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{R}^2$ be an infinite set which contains no three points on a line and no four points on a circle. problems.title: Erdos Problem #131
problems.statement_preview: Let $F(N)$ be the maximal size of $A\subseteq\{1,\ldots,N\}$ such that no $a\in A$ divides the sum of any distinct elements of $A\backslash\{a\}$. Estimate $F(N)$. In particular, is it true that $ F(N) > N^{1/2-o(1)}? $
problems.references_preview: This was studied by Erdos, Lev, Rauzy, S\'{a}ndor, and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy \cite{ELRSS99}, where they call such a property 'non-dividing', and prove the explicit bound $ F(N)<3N^{1/2}+1. $ In \cite{Er97b} Erdos credits Csaba with a construction that proves $F(N) \gg N^{1/5}$. Such a construction was also given in \cite{ELRSS99}, where it is linked to the problem of non-averaging se…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #132
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of $n$ points. Must there be two distances which occur at least once but between at most $n$ pairs of points? Must the number of such distances $\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$?
problems.references_preview: Asked by Erdos and Pach. Hopf and Pannowitz \cite{HoPa34} proved that the largest distance between points of $A$ can occur at most $n$ times, but it is unknown whether a second such distance must occur. It may be true that there are at least $n^{1-o(1)}$ many such distances. In \cite{Er97e} Erdos offers \$100 for 'any nontrivial result'. Erdos \cite{Er84c} believed that for $n…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #137
problems.statement_preview: We say that $N$ is powerful if whenever $p\mid N$ we also have $p^2\mid N$. Let $k\geq 3$. Can the product of any $k$ consecutive positive integers ever be powerful?
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Selfridge. There are infinitely many $n$ such that $n(n+1)$ is powerful (see [364]). Erdos and Selfridge \cite{ErSe75} proved that the product of $k\geq 3$ consecutive positive integers can never be a perfect power. Erdos remarked that this 'seems hopeless at present'. In \cite{Er82c} he further conjectures that, if $k$ is fixed and $n$ is sufficiently…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #138
problems.statement_preview: Let the van der Waerden number $W(k)$ be such that whenever $N\geq W(k)$ and $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ is $2$-coloured there must exist a monochromatic $k$-term arithmetic progression. Improve the bounds for $W(k)$ - for example, prove that $W(k)^{1/k}\to \infty$.
problems.references_preview: When $p$ is prime Berlekamp \cite{Be68} has proved $W(p+1)\geq p2^p$. Gowers \cite{Go01} has proved $ W(k) \leq 2^{2^{2^{2^{2^{k+9}}}}}. $ The best general lower bound is $W(k)\gg 2^k$, due to Kozik and Shabanov \cite{KoSh16}. In \cite{Er81} Erdos further asks whether $W(k+1)/W(k)\to \infty$, or $W(k+1)-W(k)\to \infty$. In \cite{Er80} Erdos asks whether $W(k)/2^k\to \infty$, a…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #141
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 3$. Are there $k$ consecutive primes in arithmetic progression?
problems.references_preview: Green and Tao \cite{GrTa08} have proved that there must always exist some $k$ primes in arithmetic progression, but these need not be consecutive. Erdos called this conjecture 'completely hopeless at present'. The existence of such progressions for small $k$ has been verified for $k\leq 10$, see the Wikipedia page. It is open, even for $k=3$, whether there are infinitely many …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #142
problems.statement_preview: Let $r_k(N)$ be the largest possible size of a subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ that does not contain any non-trivial $k$-term arithmetic progression. Prove an asymptotic formula for $r_k(N)$.
problems.references_preview: Erdos remarked this is 'probably unattackable at present'. In \cite{Er97c} Erdos offered \$1000, but given that he elsewhere offered \$5000 just for (essentially) showing that $r_k(N)=o_k(N/\log N)$, that value seems odd. In \cite{Er81} he offers \$10000, stating it is 'probably enormously difficult'. The best known upper bounds for $r_k(N)$ are due to Kelley and Meka \cite{Ke…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #143
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset (1,\infty)$ be a countably infinite set such that for all $x eq y\in A$ and integers $k\geq 1$ we have $ \lvert kx -y\rvert \geq 1. $ Does this imply that $A$ is sparse? In particular, does this imply that $ \sum_{x\in A}\frac{1}{x\log x}<\infty $ or $ \sum_{\substack{x <n\\ x\in A}}\frac{1}{x}=o(\log n)? $
problems.references_preview: Note that if $A$ is a set of integers then the condition implies that $A$ is a primitive set (that is, no element of $A$ is divisible by any other), for which the convergence of $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n\log n}$ was proved by Erdos \cite{Er35}, and the upper bound $ \sum_{n<x}\frac{1}{n}\ll \frac{\log x}{\sqrt{\log\log x}} $ was proved by Behrend \cite{Be35}. This $O(\cdot)$ bo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #145
problems.statement_preview: Let $s_1<s_2<\cdots$ be the sequence of squarefree numbers. Is it true that, for any $\alpha \geq 0$, $ \lim_{x\to \infty}\frac{1}{x}\sum_{s_n\leq x}(s_{n+1}-s_n)^\alpha $ exists?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er51} proved this for all $0\leq \alpha \leq 2$, and Hooley \cite{Ho73} extended this to all $\alpha \leq 3$. Greaves, Harman, and Huxley showed (in Chapter 11 of \cite{GHH97}) that this is true for $\alpha \leq 11/3$. Chan \cite{Ch23c} has extended this to $\alpha \leq 3.75$. Granville \cite{Gr98} proved that this follows (for all $\alpha \geq 0$) from the ABC con…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #146
problems.statement_preview: If $H$ is bipartite and is $r$-degenerate, that is, every induced subgraph of $H$ has minimum degree $\leq r$, then $ \mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^{2-1/r}. $
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Erdos and Simonovits \cite{ErSi84}. Open even for $r=2$. Alon, Krivelevich, and Sudakov \cite{AKS03} have proved $ \mathrm{ex}(n;H) \ll n^{2-1/4r}. $ They also prove the full Erdos-Simonovits conjectured bound if $H$ is bipartite and the maximum degree in one side of the bipartition is $r$. See also [113] and [147]. This problem is #43 in Extremal Graph Theory i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #148
problems.statement_preview: Let $F(k)$ be the number of solutions to $ 1= \frac{1}{n_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n_k}, $ where $1\leq n_1<\cdots<n_k$ are distinct integers. Find good estimates for $F(k)$.
problems.references_preview: The current best bounds known are $ 2^{c^{\frac{k}{\log k}}}\leq F(k) \leq c_0^{(\frac{1}{5}+o(1))2^k}, $ where $c>0$ is some absolute constant and $c_0=1.26408\cdots$ is the 'Vardi constant'. The lower bound is due to Konyagin \cite{Ko14} and the upper bound to Elsholtz and Planitzer \cite{ElPl21}. References [ElPl21] Elsholtz, Christian and Planitzer, Stefan, Sums of four an…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #149
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a graph with maximum degree $\Delta$. Is $G$ the union of at most $\tfrac{5}{4}\Delta^2$ sets of strongly independent edges (sets such that the induced subgraph is the union of vertex-disjoint edges)?
problems.references_preview: Asked by Erdos and Ne\v{s}et\v{r}il in 1985 (see \cite{FGST89}). This is equivalent to asking whether the chromatic number of the square of the line graph $L(G)^2$ is at most $\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2$. This bound would be the best possible, as witnessed by a blowup of $C_5$. The minimum number of such sets required is sometimes called the strong chromatic index of $G$. The weaker …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #151
problems.statement_preview: For a graph $G$ let $\tau(G)$ denote the minimal number of vertices that include at least one from each maximal clique of $G$ on at least two vertices (sometimes called the clique transversal number). Let $H(n)$ be maximal such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set on $H(n)$ vertices. If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices then is $ \tau(G)\leq …
problems.established_preview: No proof or counterexample obtained in this batch.
problems.references_preview: It is easy to see that $\tau(G) \leq n-\sqrt{n}$. Note also that if $G$ is triangle-free then trivially $\tau(G)\leq n-H(n)$. This is listed in \cite{Er88} as a problem of Erdos and Gallai, who were unable to make progress even assuming $G$ is $K_4$-free. There Erdos remarked that this conjecture is 'perhaps completely wrongheaded'. It later appeared as Problem 1 in \cite{EGT9…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #152
problems.statement_preview: For any $M\geq 1$, if $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ is a sufficiently large finite Sidon set then there are at least $M$ many $a\in A+A$ such that $a+1,a-1 ot\in A+A$.

problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a finite Sidon set and $A+A=\{s_1<\cdots<s_t\}$. Is it true that $ \frac{1}{t}\sum_{1\leq i<t}(s_{i+1}-s_i)^2 \to \infty $ as $\lvert A\rvert\to \infty$?
problems.references_preview: A similar problem can be asked for infinite Sidon sets.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #155
problems.statement_preview: Let $F(N)$ be the size of the largest Sidon subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$. Is it true that for every $k\geq 1$ we have $ F(N+k)\leq F(N)+1 $ for all sufficiently large $N$?
problems.references_preview: This may even hold with $k\approx \epsilon N^{1/2}$.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #156
problems.statement_preview: Does there exist a maximal Sidon set $A\subset \{1,\ldots,N\}$ of size $O(N^{1/3})$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos, S\'{a}rk"{o}zy, and S\'{o}s \cite{ESS94}. It is easy to prove that the greedy construction of a maximal Sidon set in $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ has size $\gg N^{1/3}$. Ruzsa \cite{Ru98b} constructed a maximal Sidon set of size $\ll (N\log N)^{1/3}$. See also [340]. References [ESS94] Erdos, P. and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy, A. and S\'{o}s, T., On Sum Sets of Sidon Sets, I. Jou…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #158
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ be an infinite set such that, for any $n$, there are most $2$ solutions to $a+b=n$ with $a\leq b$. Must $ \liminf_{N\to\infty}\frac{\lvert A\cap \{1,\ldots,N\}\rvert}{N^{1/2}}=0? $
problems.established_preview: Open; finite construction evidence recorded, no proof of liminf behavior.
problems.references_preview: If we replace $2$ by $1$ then $A$ is a Sidon set, for which Erdos proved this is true.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

This problem is #17 in Ramsey Theory in the graphs problem collection. References [EFRS78] Erdos, Paul and Faudree, R. J. and Rousseau, C. C. and Schelp, R. H.…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #160
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(N)$ be the smallest $k$ such that $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ can be coloured with $k$ colours so that every four-term arithmetic progression must contain at least three distinct colours. Estimate $h(N)$.

This has been discussed on MathOverflow, where LeechLattice shows $ h(N) \ll N^{2/3}. $ In the comments of this site Hunter improves this to $ h(N) \ll N^{\frac{\log 3}{\log 22}+o(1)} $ (note $\frac{\log 3}{\log 22}\approx 0.355$). The value of $c$ in the lower bound has seen a number of im…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #169
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 3$ and $f(k)$ be the supremum of $\sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n}$ as $A$ ranges over all sets of positive integers which do not contain a $k$-term arithmetic progression. Estimate $f(k)$. Is $ \lim_{k\to \infty}\frac{f(k)}{\log W(k)}=\infty $ where $W(k)$ is the van der Waerden number?
problems.references_preview: Berlekamp \cite{Be68} proved $f(k) \geq \frac{\log 2}{2}k$. Gerver \cite{Ge77} proved $ f(k) \geq (1-o(1))k\log k. $ It is trivial that $ \frac{f(k)}{\log W(k)}\geq \frac{1}{2}, $ but improving the right-hand side to any constant $>1/2$ is open. Gerver also proved (see the comments for an alternative argument of Tao) that [3] is equivalent to $f(k)$ being finite for all $k$. T…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #170
problems.statement_preview: Let $F(N)$ be the smallest possible size of $A\subset \{0,1,\ldots,N\}$ such that $\{0,1,\ldots,N\}\subset A-A$. Find the value of $ \lim_{N\to \infty}\frac{F(N)}{N^{1/2}}. $
problems.references_preview: The Sparse Ruler problem. R\'{e}dei asked whether this limit exists, which was proved by Erdos and G\'{a}l \cite{ErGa48}. Bounds on the limit were improved by Leech \cite{Le56}. The limit is known to be in the interval $[1.56,\sqrt{3}]$. The lower bound is due to Leech \cite{Le56}, the upper bound is due to Wichmann \cite{Wi63}. Computational evidence by Pegg \cite{Pe20} sugge…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #172
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that in any finite colouring of $\mathbb{N}$ there exist arbitrarily large finite $A$ such that all sums and products of distinct elements in $A$ are the same colour?
problems.references_preview: First asked by Hindman. Hindman \cite{Hi80} has proved this is false (with 7 colours) if we ask for an infinite $A$. In \cite{Er77c} Erdos asks about the case for an infinite $A$ with just $2$ colours. Moreira \cite{Mo17} has proved that in any finite colouring of $\mathbb{N}$ there exist $x,y$ such that $\{x,x+y,xy\}$ are all the same colour. Alweiss \cite{Al23} has proved th…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #173
problems.statement_preview: In any 2-coloring of `R^2`, for all but at most one triangle `T`, must there be a monochromatic congruent copy of `T`?
problems.established_preview: Background reports Shader proved the statement for a single right triangle.
problems.references_preview: For some colourings a single equilateral triangle has to be excluded, considering the colouring by alternating strips. problems.title: Erdos Problem #174
problems.statement_preview: A finite set $A\subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is called Ramsey if, for any $k\geq 1$, there exists some $d=d(A,k)$ such that in any $k$-colouring of $\mathbb{R}^d$ there exists a monochromatic copy of $A$. Characterise the Ramsey sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
problems.references_preview: Erdos, Graham, Montgomery, Rothschild, Spencer, and Straus \cite{EGMRSS73} proved that every Ramsey set is 'spherical': it lies on the surface of some sphere. Graham has conjectured that every spherical set is Ramsey. Leader, Russell, and Walters \cite{LRW12} have alternatively conjectured that a set is Ramsey if and only if it is 'subtransitive': it can be embedded in some hi…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #176
problems.statement_preview: `N(k,ell)` is the first `N` such that every `f:[1,N]->{-1,1}` has a `k`-term AP with `|sum_{P} f| >= ell`.
problems.references_preview: When $\ell=k$ this is the van der Waerden number $W(k)$ (see [138]). Spencer \cite{Sp73} has proved that if $k=2^tm$ with $m$ odd then $ N(k,1)=2^t(k-1)+1. $ Erdos and Graham write that 'no decent bound' is known even for $N(k,2)$. Erdos \cite{Er63d} proved that, for every $c>0$, $ N(k,ck)> (1+\alpha_c)^k $ where $\alpha_c\to 0$ as $c\to 0$ and $\alpha_c\to \sqrt{2}-1$ as $c\t…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #177
problems.statement_preview: Find the smallest $h(d)$ such that the following holds. There exists a function $f:\mathbb{N}\to\{-1,1\}$ such that, for every $d\geq 1$, $ \max_{P_d}\left\lvert \sum_{n\in P_d}f(n)\right\rvert\leq h(d), $ where $P_d$ ranges over all finite arithmetic progressions with common difference $d$.
problems.references_preview: Cantor, Erdos, Schreiber, and Straus \cite{Er66} proved that $h(d)\ll d!$ is possible. Van der Waerden's theorem implies that $h(d)\to \infty$. Beck \cite{Be17} has shown that $h(d) \leq d^{8+\epsilon}$ is possible for every $\epsilon>0$. Roth's famous discrepancy lower bound \cite{Ro64} implies that $h(d)\gg d^{1/2}$. References [Be17] Beck, J\'{o}zsef, A discrepancy problem:…

problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Simonovits. This is trivially true if $\mathcal{F}$ does not contain any bipartite graphs, since by the Erdos-Stone theorem if $H\in\mathcal{F}$ has minimal chromatic number $r\geq 2$ then $ \mathrm{ex}(n;H)=\mathrm{ex}(n;\mathcal{F})=\left(\frac{r-2}{r-1}+o(1)\right)\binom{n}{2}. $ Erdos and Simonovits observe that this is false for infinite families $\…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #181
problems.statement_preview: Let $Q_n$ be the $n$-dimensional hypercube graph (so that $Q_n$ has $2^n$ vertices and $n2^{n-1}$ edges). Prove that $ R(Q_n) \ll 2^n. $
problems.references_preview: Conjectured by Burr and Erdos, althouhg in \cite{Er93} Erdos says the behaviour of $R(Q_n)$ was considered by himself and S\'{o}s, who could not decide whether $R(Q_n)/2^n\to \infty$ or not. problems.title: Erdos Problem #183
problems.statement_preview: Let $R(3;k)$ be the minimal $n$ such that if the edges of $K_n$ are coloured with $k$ colours then there must exist a monochromatic triangle. Determine $ \lim_{k\to \infty}R(3;k)^{1/k}. $
problems.references_preview: Erdos offers \$100 for showing that this limit is finite. An easy pigeonhole argument shows that $ R(3;k)\leq 2+k(R(3;k-1)-1), $ from which $R(3;k)\leq \lceil e k!\rceil$ immediately follows. problems.title: Erdos Problem #367

problems.established_preview: The background records van Doorn's observation: - for `k<=2`, `<< n^2` is trivial; - for every `k>=3`, the `<<_k n^2` claim fails; - in fact for `k=3`, `prod_{n <= m < n+3} B_2(m) >> n^2 log n` infinitely often. problems.title: Erdos Problem #406
problems.statement_preview: Are there only finitely many powers of `2` whose base-`3` expansion uses only digits `{0,1}`?
problems.references_preview: The only examples seem to be $1$, $4=1+3$, and $256=1+3+3^2+3^5$. If we only allow the digits $1$ and $2$ then $2^{15}$ seems to be the largest such power of $2$. This would imply via Kummer's theorem that $ 3\mid \binom{2^{k+1}}{2^k} $ for all large $k$. Saye \cite{Sa22} has computed that $2^n$ contains every possible ternary digit for $16\leq n \leq 5.9\times 10^{21}$. This …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #408
problems.statement_preview: With `f(n)=\min\{k:\phi_k(n)=1\}`, study whether `f(n)/\log n` has a distribution / normal order, and how large the largest prime factor of `\phi_k(n)` is for `k\approx\log\log n`.
problems.references_preview: Pillai \cite{Pi29} was the first to investigate this function, and proved $ \log_3 n < f(n) < \log_2 n $ for all large $n$. Shapiro \cite{Sh50} proved that $f(n)$ is essentially multiplicative. Erdos, Granville, Pomerance, and Spiro \cite{EGPS90} have proved that the answer to the first two questions is yes, conditional on a form of the Elliott-Halberstam conjecture. It is lik…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #409
problems.statement_preview: For `T(n)=\phi(n)+1`, how many iterations are needed to hit a prime? Can infinitely many `n` hit the same prime? What is the density of integers reaching a fixed prime?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Finucane. One can also ask similar questions about $n\mapsto \sigma(n)-1$: do iterates of this always reach a prime? If so, how soon? (It is easily seen that iterates of this cannot reach the same prime infinitely often, since they are non-decreasing.) This problem is somewhat ambiguously phrased. Let $F(n)$ count the number of iterations of $n\mapsto \phi(n)+1$ b…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #410
problems.statement_preview: For `\sigma_1(n)=\sigma(n)` and `\sigma_k(n)=\sigma(\sigma_{k-1}(n))`, is it true for every `n>=2` that `\lim_{k\to\infty}\sigma_k(n)^{1/k}=\infty`?
problems.references_preview: This is discussed in problem B9 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #411
problems.statement_preview: For `g(n)=n+\phi(n)` and iterates `g_k`, determine pairs `(n,r)` such that `g_{k+r}(n)=2g_k(n)` for all large `k`.
problems.references_preview: The known solutions to $g_{k+2}(n)=2g_k(n)$ are $n=10$ and $n=94$. Selfridge and Weintraub found solutions to $g_{k+9}(n)=9g_k(n)$ and Weintraub found $ g_{k+25}(3114)=729g_k(3114) $ for all $k\geq 6$. Steinerberger \cite{St25} has observed that, for $r=2$, this problem is equivalent to asking for solutions to $ \phi(n)+\phi(n+\phi(n))=n, $ and has shown that if this holds the…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #412
problems.statement_preview: For iterates of the divisor-sum map `sigma_i`, does every pair `m,n>=2` have some collision `sigma_i(m)=sigma_j(n)`?
problems.established_preview: Background records numerical evidence (Selfridge) suggesting the answer is no.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er79d} Erdos attributes this conjecture to van Wijngaarden, who told it to Erdos in the 1950s. That is, there is (eventually) only one possible sequence that the iterated sum of divisors function can settle on. Selfridge reports numerical evidence which suggests the answer is no, but Erdos and Graham write 'it seems unlikely that anything can be proved about this in t…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #413
problems.statement_preview: For `\omega(n)` barriers (`m+\omega(m)\le n` for all `m<n`), are there infinitely many? Also, does some weakened `\epsilon\omega(m)` version have infinitely many barriers? Related questions for `\Omega` are natural too.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er79} Erdos calls such an $n$ a 'barrier' for $\omega$. Some other natural number theoretic functions (such as $\phi$ and $\sigma$) have no barriers because they increase too rapidly. Erdos believed that $\omega$ should have infinitely many barriers. problems.title: Erdos Problem #414
problems.statement_preview: For iterates of `h(n)=n+tau(n)`, do all starting values eventually merge into a single trajectory class?
problems.established_preview: - no proof or counterexample obtained in this attempt.
problems.references_preview: Asked by Spiro. That is, there is (eventually) only one possible sequence that the iterations of $n\mapsto h(n)$ can settle on. Erdos and Graham believed the answer is yes. Similar questions can be asked by the iterates of many other functions. See also [412] and [413].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

Weisenberg has observed that the same questions could be asked for ordering patterns which allow equality (indeed, the final problem only makes sense if we allow equality). References [Er36b] Erdos…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #416
problems.statement_preview: Let $V(x)$ count the number of $n\leq x$ such that $\phi(m)=n$ is solvable. Does $V(2x)/V(x)\to 2$? Is there an asymptotic formula for $V(x)$?
problems.references_preview: Pillai \cite{Pi29} proved $V(x)=o(x)$. Erdos \cite{Er35b} proved $V(x)=x(\log x)^{-1+o(1)}$. The behaviour of $V(x)$ is now almost completely understood. Maier and Pomerance \cite{MaPo88} proved $ V(x)=\frac{x}{\log x}e^{(C+o(1))(\log\log\log x)^2}, $ for some explicit constant $C>0$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #417
problems.statement_preview: Let $ V'(x)=\#\{\phi(m) : 1\leq m\leq x\} $ and $ V(x)=\#\{\phi(m) \leq x : 1\leq m\}. $ Does $\lim V(x)/V'(x)$ exist? Is it $>1$?
problems.references_preview: It is trivial that $V'(x) \leq V(x)$. In \cite{Er98} Erdos suggests the limit may be infinite. See also [416]. problems.title: Erdos Problem #420
problems.statement_preview: If $\tau(n)$ counts the number of divisors of $n$ then let $ F(f,n)=\frac{\tau((n+\lfloor f(n)\rfloor)!)}{\tau(n!)}. $ Is it true that $ \lim_{n\to \infty}F((\log n)^C,n)=\infty $ for large $C$? problems.references_preview: Erdos and Graham write that it is easy to show that $\lim F(n^{1/2},n)=\infty$, and in fact the $n^{1/2}$ can be replaced by $n^{1/2-c}$ for some small constant $c>0$. Erdos, Graham, Ivi\'{c}, and Pomerance \cite{EGIP96} have proved that $ \liminf F(c\log n, n) = 1 $ for any $c>0$, and $ \lim F(n^{4/9},n)=\infty. $ (The exponent $4/9$ can be improved slightly.) They also prove…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #421
problems.statement_preview: Does there exist a density-one sequence `d_1<d_2<...` such that all products `prod_{u<=i<=v} d_i` are distinct?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #422
problems.statement_preview: For Hofstadter's recursion `f(1)=f(2)=1`, `f(n)=f(n-f(n-1))+f(n-f(n-2))`, ask if infinitely many integers are missed and determine behavior.

In \cite{ErGr80} (and in Guy's book) this problem as written is asking for whether almost all integers appear in this sequence, but the answer to this is trivially no (as pointed out to me…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #428

problems.title: Erdos Problem #431
problems.statement_preview: Are there two infinite sets $A$ and $B$ such that $A+B$ agrees with the set of prime numbers up to finitely many exceptions?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Ostmann, sometimes known as the 'inverse Goldbach problem'. The answer is surely no. The best result in this direction is due to Elsholtz and Harper \cite{ElHa15}, who showed that if $A,B$ are such sets then for all large $x$ we must have $ \frac{x^{1/2}}{\log x\log\log x} \ll \lvert A \cap [1,x]\rvert \ll x^{1/2}\log\log x $ and similarly for $B$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #436
problems.statement_preview: If $p$ is a prime and $k,m\geq 2$ then let $r(k,m,p)$ be the minimal $r$ such that $r,r+1,\ldots,r+m-1$ are all $k$th power residues modulo $p$. problems.established_preview: The first question (`\Lambda(k,2)` finite for all `k`) is already solved in background (Hildebrand, 1991).

problems.title: Erdos Problem #445
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that, for any $c>1/2$, if $p$ is a sufficiently large prime then, for any $n\geq 0$, there exist $a,b\in(n,n+p^c)$ such that $ab\equiv 1\pmod{p}$?
problems.references_preview: Heilbronn (unpublished) proved this for $c$ sufficiently close to $1$. Heath-Brown \cite{He00} used Kloosterman sums to prove this for all $c>3/4$. Cambie has observed that if this is intended to hold for all $x$ then, provided $ \epsilon(\log n)^\delta (\log\log n)^{3/2}\to \infty $ as $n\to \infty$, where $\delta=0.086\cdots$, there is no such $y$, which follows from an averaging argument and the work of Ford \cite{Fo08}. problems.title: Erdos Problem #451
problems.statement_preview: `n_k` is the smallest integer `>2k` such that `prod_{1<=i<=k}(n_k-i)` has no prime factor in `(k,2k)`. Estimate `n_k`.
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Graham write 'we can prove $n_k>k^{1+c}$ but no doubt much more is true'. In \cite{Er79d} Erdos writes that probably $n_k<e^{o(k)}$ but $n_k>k^d$ for all constant $d$. Adenwalla observes that an easy upper bound is $n_k\leq \prod_{k<p<2k}p=e^{O(k)}$. References [Er79d] Erdos, P., Some unconventional problems in number theory. Acta Math. Acad. Sci. Hungar. (1979), 71-…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #452
problems.statement_preview: Find the largest interval I subset [x,2x] on which omega(n)>log log n for all n in I.
problems.established_preview: Background provides: - density of such n is 1/2; - CRT construction yields interval length at least (1+o(1)) log x/(log log x)^2.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er37} proved that the density of integers $n$ with $\omega(n)>\log\log n$ is $1/2$. The Chinese remainder theorem implies that there is such an interval with $ \lvert I\rvert \geq (1+o(1))\frac{\log x}{(\log\log x)^2}. $ It could be true that there is such an interval of length $(\log x)^{k}$ for arbitrarily large $k$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #454
problems.statement_preview: For `f(n)=min_{i<n}(p_{n+i}+p_{n-i})`, is `limsup (f(n)-2p_n)=infty`?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #456

Erdos \cite{Er79e} writes it is 'easy to show' that for infinitely many $n$ we have $m_n <p_n$, and that $m_n/n\to \infty$ for almost all $n$. van Doorn in the comments has noted that if $n=2^{2k+1}$ then $m_n\leq 2n$ and $p_n\geq 2n+1$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #457

problems.title: Erdos Problem #460
problems.statement_preview: Let $a_0=0$ and $a_1=1$, and in general define $a_k$ to be the least integer $>a_{k-1}$ for which $(n-a_k,n-a_i)=1$ for all $0\leq i<k$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #461

problems.title: Erdos Problem #463

The best known lower bound is due to Grebennikov, Sagdeev, Semchankau, and Vasilevskii \cite{GSSV24}, $ \lvert A_p\rvert \geq (\sqrt{2}-o(1))p^{1/2}, $ which follows from proving that $\lvert A_pA_p\rvert=(1+o(1))p$. Wilson's theorem implies $(p-2)!\equiv 1\pmod{p}$, and hence $\lvert A_p\rvert\le…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #479
problems.statement_preview: For fixed `k != 1`, are there infinitely many `n` with `2^n \equiv k (mod n)`?
problems.established_preview: Background reports proofs for `k=2^i` (`i>=1`) and for `k=-1`. A local scan up to `n<=10^6` found first witnesses for many small `k` (examples: `k=5` at `n=19147`, `k=11` at `n=262279`), while not finding one for `k=3` within that bound.
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Graham. It is easy to see that $2^n ot\equiv 1\mod{n}$ for all $n>1$, so the restriction $k eq 1$ is necessary. Erdos and Graham report that Graham, Lehmer, and Lehmer have proved this for $k=2^i$ for $i\geq 1$, or if $k=-1$, but I cannot find such a paper. Tang has written a short note giving a proof for this case. As an indication of the difficulty, when $k=3…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #483
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(k)$ be the minimal $N$ such that if $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ is $k$-coloured then there is a monochromatic solution to $a+b=c$. Estimate $f(k)$. In particular, is it true that $f(k) < c^k$ for some constant $c>0$?
problems.references_preview: The values of $f(k)$ are known as Schur numbers. The best-known bounds for large $k$ are $ (380)^{k/5}-O(1)\leq f(k) \leq \lfloor(e-\tfrac{1}{24}) k!\rfloor-1. $ The lower bound is due to Ageron, Casteras, Pellerin, Portella, Rimmel, and Tomasik \cite{ACPPRT21} (improving previous bounds of Exoo \cite{Ex94} and Fredricksen and Sweet \cite{FrSw00}) and the upper bound is due to…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #486

The problem considers logarithmic density since Besicovitch \cite{Be34} showed examples exist without a natural density, even when $X_n=\{0\}$ for all $n\in A$. This is a generalisation of [2…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #500
problems.statement_preview: What is $\mathrm{ex}_3(n,K_4^3)$? That is, the largest number of $3$-edges which can placed on $n$ vertices so that there exists no $K_4^3$, a set of 4 vertices which is covered by all 4 possible $3$-edges.
problems.references_preview: A problem of Tur\'{a}n. Tur\'{a}n observed that dividing the vertices into three equal parts $X_1,X_2,X_3$, and taking the edges to be those triples that either have exactly one vertex in each part or two vertices in $X_i$ and one vertex in $X_{i+1}$ (where $X_4=X_1$) shows that $ \mathrm{ex}_3(n,K_4^3)\geq\left(\frac{5}{9}+o(1)\right)\binom{n}{3}. $ This is probably the truth…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #501
problems.statement_preview: For every $x\in\mathbb{R}$ let $A_x\subset \mathbb{R}$ be a bounded set with outer measure $<1$. Must there exist an infinite independent set, that is, some infinite $X\subseteq \mathbb{R}$ such that $x ot\in A_y$ for all $x eq y\in X$? If the sets $A_x$ are closed and have measure $<1$, then must there exist an independent set of size $3$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Hajnal \cite{ErHa60} proved the existence of arbitrarily large finite independent sets (under the assumptions in the first problem). Gladysz \cite{Gl62} proved the existence of an independent set of size $2$ under the assumptions of the the second question. Hechler \cite{He72} has shown the answer to the first question is no, assuming the continuum hypothesis. Newels…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #503
problems.statement_preview: What is the size of the largest $A\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ such that every three points from $A$ determine an isosceles triangle? That is, for any three points $x,y,z$ from $A$, at least two of the distances $\lvert x-y\rvert,\lvert y-z\rvert,\lvert x-z\rvert$ are equal.
problems.references_preview: When $d=2$ the answer is $6$ (due to Kelly \cite{ErKe47} - an alternative proof is given by Kov\'{a}cs \cite{Ko24c}). When $d=3$ the answer is $8$ (due to Croft \cite{Cr62}). The best upper bound known in general is due to Blokhuis \cite{Bl84} who showed that $ \lvert A\rvert \leq \binom{d+2}{2}. $ Alweiss has observed a lower bound of $\binom{d+1}{2}$ follows from considering…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #507

problems.title: Erdos Problem #508

problems.references_preview: The Hadwiger-Nelson problem. Let $\chi$ be the chromatic number of the plane. An equilateral triangle trivially shows that $\chi\geq 3$. There are several small graphs that show $\chi\geq 4$ (in particular the Moser spindle and Golomb graph). The best bounds currently known are $ 5 \leq \chi \leq 7. $ The lower bound is due to de Grey \cite{dG18}. The upper bound can be seen b…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #524

problems.title: Erdos Problem #528
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n,k)$ count the number of self-avoiding walks of $n$ steps (beginning at the origin) in $\mathbb{Z}^k$ (i.e. those walks which do not intersect themselves). Determine $ C_k=\lim_{n\to\infty}f(n,k)^{1/n}. $

Kesten \cite{Ke63} proved that $C_k=2k-1-1/2k+O(1/k^2)$, and more precise asymptotics are given by Clisby, Liang, and Slade \cite{CLS07}. problems.title: Erdos Problem #529

problems.title: Erdos Problem #564

problems.title: Erdos Problem #566

problems.references_preview: In other words, is $G$ Ramsey size linear? This fails for a graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and $2n-2$ edges (for example with $H=K_n$). Erdos, Faudree, Rousseau, and Schelp \cite{EFRS93} have shown that any graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and at most $n+1$ edges is Ramsey size linear. Implies [567]. problems.statement_preview: Is every sufficiently large integer representable as `n = a p^2 + b` with prime `p`, `a>=1`, `0<=b<p`?
problems.references_preview: The sieve of Eratosthenes implies that almost all integers are of this form, and the Brun-Selberg sieve implies the number of exceptions in $[1,x]$ is $\ll x/(\log x)^c$ for some constant $c>0$. Erdos \cite{Er79} believed it is 'rather unlikely' that all large integers are of this form. What if the condition that $p$ is prime is omitted? Selfridge and Wagstaff made a 'prelimin…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #677
problems.statement_preview: Let $M(n,k)=[n+1,\ldots,n+k]$ be the least common multiple of $\{n+1,\ldots,n+k\}$. Is it true that for all $m\geq n+k$ $ M(n,k) eq M(m,k)? $
problems.references_preview: The Thue-Siegel theorem implies that, for fixed $k$, there are only finitely many $m,n$ such that $m\geq n+k$ and $M(n,k)=M(m,k)$. In general, how many solutions does $M(n,k)=M(m,l)$ have when $m\geq n+k$ and $l>1$? Erdos expects very few (and none when $l\geq k$). The only solutions Erdos knew were $M(4,3)=M(13,2)$ and $M(3,4)=M(19,2)$. In \cite{Er79d} Erdos conjectures the s…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #679
problems.statement_preview: With `omega(n)` = number of distinct prime factors: 1. For fixed `epsilon>0`, are there infinitely many `n` such that `omega(n-k) < (1+epsilon) log k / log log k` for all sufficiently large `k<n`? 2. Is the stronger form `omega(n-k) < log k / log log k + O(1)` false?
problems.established_preview: The background records that (in comments) DottedCalculator disproved the stronger form by proving: for all sufficiently large `n`, there exists `k<n` such that `omega(n-k) >= log k / log log k + c * log k / (log log k)^2` for some constant `c>0`. This is stronger than merely violating `+O(1)`, so the second subquestion is answered in the affirmative: the stronger version is fa…
problems.references_preview: One can ask similar questions for $\Omega$, the number of prime factors with multiplicity, where $\log k/\log\log k$ is replaced by $\log_2k$. See also [248] and [413]. In the comments DottedCalculator has disproved the second stronger version, proving that in fact for all large $n$ there exists $k<n$ such that $ \omega(n-k)\geq \frac{\log k}{\log\log k} + c\frac{\log k}{(\log…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #680
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that, for all sufficiently large $n$, there exists some $k$ such that $ p(n+k)>k^2+1, $ where $p(m)$ denotes the least prime factor of $m$? Can one prove this is false if we replace $k^2+1$ by $e^{(1+\epsilon)\sqrt{k}}+C_\epsilon$, for all $\epsilon>0$, where $C_\epsilon>0$ is some constant?
problems.references_preview: This follows from 'plausible assumptions on the distribution of primes' (as does the question with $k^2$ replaced by $k^d$ for any $d$); the challenge is to prove this unconditionally. Erdos observed that Cramer's conjecture $ \limsup_{k\to \infty} \frac{p_{k+1}-p_k}{(\log k)^2}=1 $ implies that for all $\epsilon>0$ and all sufficiently large $n$ there exists some $k$ such tha…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #681
problems.statement_preview: For large `n`, is there `k` with `n+k` composite and least prime factor `p(n+k) > k^2`?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #683

Erdos \cite{Er55d} proved that there exists some $c>0$ such that, whenever $k\leq n/2$, $ P\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)\gg k\log k. $ Erdos \cite{Er79d} writes it 'seems certain' that this holds for every $c>0$, with only a finite number of exceptions (depending on $c$). Standard he…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #684
problems.statement_preview: For the decomposition of `binom(n,k)` into small-prime and large-prime parts, define `f(n)` as first `k` where the small-prime part exceeds `n^2`. Bound `f(n)`.

Mahler's theorem implies $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$, but is i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #685
problems.statement_preview: Estimate number of distinct prime divisors of `binom(n,k)` in the range `n^epsilon < k <= n^{1-epsilon}` and possible extensions.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #686
problems.statement_preview: Can every integer `N>=2` be represented as a ratio of two products of `k` consecutive integers with `k>=2` and `m>=n+k`?

problems.title: Erdos Problem #687
problems.statement_preview: Let $Y(x)$ be the maximal $y$ such that there exists a choice of congruence classes $a_p$ for all primes $p\leq x$ such that every integer in $[1,y]$ is congruent to at least one of the $a_p\pmod{p}$. Give good estimates for $Y(x)$. In particular, can one prove that $Y(x)=o(x^2)$ or even $Y(x)\ll x^{1+o(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: This function (associated with Jacobsthal) is closely related to the problem of gaps between primes (see [4]). The best known upper bound is due to Iwaniec \cite{Iw78}, $ Y(x) \ll x^2. $ The best lower bound is due to Ford, Green, Konyagin, Maynard, and Tao \cite{FGKMT18}, $ Y(x) \gg x\frac{\log x\log\log\log x}{\log\log x}, $ improving on a previous bound of Rankin \cite{Ra38…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #688
problems.statement_preview: For covering `[1,n]` by chosen residue classes modulo primes in `(n^{epsilon_n}, n]`, estimate `epsilon_n`; in particular, is `epsilon_n=o(1)`?

problems.references_preview: Erdos could prove $ \epsilon_n \gg \frac{\log\log\log n}{\log\log n}. $ See also [687] and [689].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #689
