problems.title: Erdos Problem #690
problems.statement_preview: Let $d_k(p)$ be the density of those integers whose $k$th smallest prime factor is $p$ (i.e. if $p_1<p_2<\cdots$ are the primes dividing $n$ then $p_k=p$). For fixed $k\geq 1$ is $d_k(p)$ unimodular in $p$? That is, it first increases in $p$ until its maximum then decreases.
problems.references_preview: Erdos believes that this is not possible, but could not disprove it. He could show that $p_k$ is about $e^{e^k}$ for almost all $n$, but the maximal value of $d_k(p)$ is assumed for much smaller values of $p$, at $ p=e^{(1+o(1))k}. $ A similar question can be asked if we consider the density of integers whose $k$th smallest divisor is $d$. Erdos could show that this function i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #691
problems.statement_preview: Given $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ let $M_A=\{ n \geq 1 : a\mid n\textrm{ for some }a\in A\}$ be the set of multiples of $A$. Find a necessary and sufficient condition on $A$ for $M_A$ to have density $1$.
problems.established_preview: Partial subclass behavior explored; general characteri
problems.references_preview: A sequence $A$ for which $M_A$ has density $1$ is called a Behrend sequence. If $A$ is a set of prime numbers (or, more generally, a set of pairwise coprime integers without $1$) then a necessary and sufficient condition is that $\sum_{p\in A}\frac{1}{p}=\infty$. The general situation is more complicated. For example suppose $A$ is the union of $(n_k,(1+\eta_k)n_k)\cap \mathbb…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #693
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 2$ and $n$ be sufficiently large depending on $k$. Let $A=\{a_1<a_2<\cdots \}$ be the set of those integers in $[n,n^k]$ which have a divisor in $(n,2n)$. Estimate $ \max_{i} a_{i+1}-a_i. $ Is this $\leq (\log n)^{O(1)}$? ", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #694
problems.statement_preview: For totient fibers, with f_max(n) and f_min(n) the largest/smallest m such that phi(m)=n, study max_{n<=x} f_max(n)/f_min(n).
problems.established_preview: Background links this to Carmichael's problem (existence of n with unique preimage, i.e. ratio 1), and records Erdos's implication: if one such n exists, then infinitely many exist.
problems.references_preview: Carmichael has asked whether there is an integer $n$ for which $\phi(m)=n$ has exactly one solution, that is, $\frac{f_{\max}(n)}{f_{\min}(n)}=1$. Erdos has proved that if such an $n$ exists then there must be infinitely many such $n$.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #695
problems.statement_preview: Let $p_1<p_2<\cdots$ be a sequence of primes such that $p_{i+1}\equiv 1\pmod{p_i}$. Is it true that $ \lim_k p_k^{1/k}=\infty? $ Does there exist such a sequence with $ p_k \leq \exp(k(\log k)^{1+o(1)})? $
problems.references_preview: Such a sequence is sometimes called a prime chain. If we take the obvious 'greedy' chain with $2=p_1$ and $p_{i+1}$ is the smallest prime $\equiv 1\pmod{p_i}$ then Linnik's theorem implies that this sequence grows like $ p_k \leq e^{e^{O(k)}}. $ It is conjectured that, for any prime $p$, there is a prime $p'\leq p(\log p)^{O(1)}$ which is congruent to $1\pmod{p}$, which would …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #696
problems.statement_preview: For n, define h(n) as max length of prime chain p_{i+1}≡1 (mod p_i) inside divisors of n, and H(n) similarly with integer divisors. Estimate h(n), H(n), and ask if H(n)/h(n)->infinity for almost all n.
problems.established_preview: Background records that h(n)->infinity for almost all n (proved in comments), and states Erdos's belief that normal order of h(n) is log_*(n).
problems.references_preview: Erdos writes it is 'easy to see' that $h(n)\to \infty$ for almost all $n$ (which is proved in the comments by van Doorn), and believed he could show that the normal order of $h(n)$ is $\log_*(n)$ (the iterated logarithm). See also [695].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #700
problems.statement_preview: For f(n)=min_{1<k<=n/2} gcd(n, C(n,k)), answer three questions on structure and si
problems.established_preview: Background provides: - general bound f(n)<=n/P(n), hence f(n)<=(1+o(1)) n/log n; - equality f(n)=n/P(n) for n product of two primes (and n=30); - infinitely many composite n with f(n)>n^{1/2} (e.g. n=p^2).
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Szekeres. It is easy to see that $f(n)\leq n/P(n)$ for composite $n$, since if $j=p^k$ where $p^k\mid n$ and $p^{k+1} mid n$ then $\textrm{gcd}\left(n,\binom{n}{j}\right)=n/p^k$. This implies $ f(n) \leq (1+o(1))\frac{n}{\log n}. $ It is known that $f(n)=n/P(n)$ when $n$ is the product of two primes. Another example is $n=30$. For the second problem, it …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #701
problems.statement_preview: Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a family of sets closed under taking subsets (i.e. if $B\subseteq A\in\mathcal{F}$ then $B\in \mathcal{F}$). There exists some element $x$ such that whenever $\mathcal{F}'\subseteq \mathcal{F}$ is an intersecting subfamily we have $ \lvert \mathcal{F}'\rvert \leq \lvert \{ A\in \mathcal{F} : x\in A\}\rvert. $
problems.references_preview: A problem of Chv\'{a}tal \cite{Ch74}, who proved it replacing the closed under subsets condition with the (stronger) condition that, assuming all sets in $\mathcal{F}$ are subsets of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$, whenever $A\in \mathcal{F}$ and there is an injection $f:B\to A$ such that $x\leq f(x)$ for all $x\in B$, then $B\in \mathcal{F}$. Sterboul \cite{St74} proved this when, letting …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #704
problems.statement_preview: Let $G_n$ be the unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^n$, with two vertices joined by an edge if and only if the distance between them is $1$. Estimate the chromatic number $\chi(G_n)$. Does it grow exponentially in $n$? Does $ \lim_{n\to \infty}\chi(G_n)^{1/n} $ exist?
problems.references_preview: A generalisation of the Hadwiger-Nelson problem (which addresses $n=2$). Frankl and Wilson \cite{FrWi81} proved exponential growth: $ \chi(G_n) \geq (1+o(1))1.2^n. $ The trivial colouring (by tiling with cubes) gives $ \chi(G_n) \leq (2+\sqrt{n})^n. $ Larman and Rogers \cite{LaRo72} improved this to $ \chi(G_n) \leq (3+o(1))^n, $ and conjecture the truth may be $(2^{3/2}+o(1))…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #705
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a finite unit distance graph in $\mathbb{R}^2$ (i.e. the vertices are a finite collection of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and there is an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is $1$). Is there some $k$ such that if $G$ has girth $\geq k$ (i.e. $G$ contains no cycles of length $<k$) then $\chi(G)\leq 3$?
problems.references_preview: The maximal value of $\chi(G)$ (without a girth condition) is the Hadwiger-Nelson problem. There are unit distance graphs (e.g. the Moser spindle) with $\chi(G)=4$ of girth $3$. de Grey \cite{dG18} has constructed a unit distance graph $G$ with $\chi(G)=5$. (I do not know what the largest girth achieved is by these recent constructions.) Wormald \cite{Wo79} has constructed a u…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #706
problems.statement_preview: L(r): maximum possible chromatic number for planar distance graphs built from a point set P and r allowed distances. Estimate L(r), especially whether L(r)<=r^{O(1)}.
problems.references_preview: The case $r=1$ is the Hadwiger-Nelson problem, for which it is known that $5\leq L(1)\leq 7$. See also [508], [704], and [705].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #708
problems.statement_preview: Let $g(n)$ be minimal such that for any $A\subseteq [2,\infty)\cap \mathbb{N}$ with $\lvert A\rvert =n$ and any set $I$ of $\max(A)$ consecutive integers there exists some $B\subseteq I$ with $\lvert B\rvert=g(n)$ such that $ \prod_{a\in A} a \mid \prod_{b\in B}b. $ Is it true that $ g(n) \leq (2+o(1))n? $ Or perhaps even $g(n)\leq 2n$?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Sur\'{a}nyi \cite{ErSu59}, who proved that $g(n) \geq (2-o(1))n$, and that $g(3)=4$. Their lower bound construction takes $A$ as the set of $p_ip_j$ for $i eq j$, where $p_1<\cdots <p_\ell$ is some set of primes such that $2p_1^2>p_\ell^2$. Gallai was the first to consider problems of this type, and observed that $g(2)=2$ and $g(3)\geq 4$. In \cite{Er92c…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #709
problems.statement_preview: `f(n)` is the minimal interval-length factor forcing distinct divisibility matches for any `n`-set `A`.
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Sur\'{a}nyi \cite{ErSu59}, who proved $ (\log n)^c \ll f(n) \ll n^{1/2} $ for some constant $c>0$. See also [708]. References [ErSu59] Erdos, P\'{a}l and Sur\'{a}nyi, J\'{a}nos, Bemerkungen zu einer Aufgabe eines mathematischen {W}ettbewerbs. Mat. Lapok (1959), 39-48.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n,m)$ be minimal such that in $(m,m+f(n,m))$ there exist distinct integers $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ such that $k\mid a_k$ for all $1\leq k\leq n$. Prove that $ \max_m f(n,m) \leq n^{1+o(1)} $ and that $ \max_m (f(n,m)-f(n,n))\to \infty. $

problems.title: Erdos Problem #712
problems.statement_preview: Determine `ex_r(n,K_k^r)/C(n,r)` for fixed `k>r>2`.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #713

problems.title: Erdos Problem #714
problems.statement_preview: For fixed `r`, ask whether `ex(n;K_{r,r}) >> n^{2-1/r}`.
problems.references_preview: K"{o}v\'{a}ri, S\'{o}s, and Tur\'{a}n \cite{KST54} proved $ \mathrm{ex}(n; K_{r,r}) \ll n^{2-1/r} $ for all $r\geq 2$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #724

Chowla, Erdos, and Straus \cite{CES60} proved $f(n) \gg n^{1/91}$. Wilson \cite{Wi74} proved $f(n) \gg n^{1/17}$. Beth \cite{Be83c} proved $f(n) \gg n^{1/14.8}$. The sequence of $f(n)$ is A001438 in the OEIS. Refer…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #725
problems.statement_preview: Give an asymptotic formula for the number of k x n Latin rectangles.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #727
problems.statement_preview: For fixed `k>=2`, does `(n+k)!^2` divide `(2n)!` for infinitely many `n`?
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Erdos, Graham, Ruzsa, and Straus \cite{EGRS75}. It is open even for $k=2$. Balakran \cite{Ba29} proved this holds for $k=1$ - that is, $(n+1)^2\mid \binom{2n}{n}$ for infinitely many $n$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #731

problems.title: Erdos Problem #740
problems.statement_preview: For infinite cardinal `m`, if `chi(G)=m`, must `G` contain a subgraph of chromatic number `m` with no odd cycle of length `<= r`?

problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Hajnal. R"{o}dl proved this is true if $\mathfrak{m}=\aleph_0$ and $r=3$ (see [108] for the finitary version). problems.title: Erdos Problem #741
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be such that $A+A$ has positive density. Can one always decompose $A=A_1\sqcup A_2$ such that $A_1+A_1$ and $A_2+A_2$ both have positive density? Is there a basis $A$ of order $2$ such that if $A=A_1\sqcup A_2$ then $A_1+A_1$ and $A_2+A_2$ cannot both have bounded gaps?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Burr and Erdos. Erdos \cite{Er94b} thought he could construct a basis as in the second question, but 'could never quite finish the proof'. References [Er94b] Erdos, Paul, Some problems in number theory, combinatorics and combinatorial geometry. Math. Pannon. (1994), 261-269.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #750

This follows from a result of Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di \cite{EHS82}, as described by msellke in the comments. In \cite{ErHa67b} Erdos and Hajnal prove this for $f(m)\geq cm$ for all $c>1/4$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #757

problems.title: Erdos Problem #761
problems.statement_preview: Ask whether large chromatic number forces large dichromatic number, and whether large cochromatic number contains a subgraph with large dichromatic number.
problems.references_preview: The first question is due to Erdos and Neumann-Lara. The second question is due to Erdos and Gimbel. A positive answer to the second question implies a positive answer to the first via the bound mentioned in [760].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #766
problems.statement_preview: For fixed (k,l), f(n;k,l)=min ex(n;G) over k-vertex l-edge graphs G. Estimate in k<l<=k^2/4 and ask strict monotonicity in l for fixed k, large n.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #768

problems.title: Erdos Problem #769
problems.statement_preview: Let $c(n)$ be minimal such that if $k\geq c(n)$ then the $n$-dimensional unit cube can be decomposed into $k$ homothetic $n$-dimensional cubes. Give good bounds for $c(n)$ - in particular, is it true that $c(n) \gg n^n$?
problems.references_preview: A problem first investigated by Hadwiger, who proved the lower bound $ c(n) \geq 2^n+2^{n-1}. $ It is easy to see that $c(2)=6$. Meier conjectured $c(3)=48$. Burgess and Erdos \cite{Er74b} proved $ c(n) \ll n^{n+1}. $ Erdos wrote 'I am certain that if $n+1$ is a prime then $c(n)>n^n$.' Hudelson \cite{Hu98} proved that if $(2^n-1,3^n-1)=1$ then $c(n) < 6^n$, and in general $c(n…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #770
problems.statement_preview: Define h(n) as the minimal H such that 2^n-1,3^n-1,...,H^n-1 are mutually coprime. Questions ask about densities of h(n)=p, liminf h(n), and a large-prime divisor criterion.
problems.established_preview: Background records: - h(n)=n+1 iff n+1 is prime. - h(n) is unbounded on odd n.
problems.references_preview: It is easy to see that $h(n)=n+1$ if and only if $n+1$ is prime, and that $h(n)$ is unbounded for odd $n$. It is probably true that $h(n)=3$ for infinitely many $n$. See also [820].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #773

problems.title: Erdos Problem #778

problems.references_preview: A question of Brown, Erdos, and Freedman \cite{BEF90}. It is a classical fact that the squares do not contain arithmetic progressions of length $4$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #821

problems.references_preview: Pillai proved that $\limsup g(n)=\infty$ and Erdos \cite{Er35b} proved that there exists some constant $c>0$ such that $g(n) >n^c$ for infinitely many $n$. This conjecture would follow if we knew that, for every $\epsilon>0$, there are $\gg_\epsilon \frac{x}{\log x}$ many primes $p<x$ such that all prime factors of $p-1$ are $<p^\epsilon$. The best known bound is that there ar…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #824
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(x)$ count the number of integers $1\leq a<b<x$ such that $(a,b)=1$ and $\sigma(a)=\sigma(b)$, where $\sigma$ is the sum of divisors function. Is it true that $h(x)>x^{2-o(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er74b} proved that $\limsup h(x)/x= \infty$, and claimed a similar proof for this problem. A complete proof that $h(x)/x\to \infty$ was provided by Pollack and Pomerance \cite{PoPo16}. A similar question can be asked if we replace the condition $(a,b)=1$ with the condition that $a$ and $b$ are squarefree. Weisenberg suggests another variant, with the condition that…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #825

problems.references_preview: A problem of Benkoski and Erdos. In other words, this problem asks for an upper bound for the abundancy index of weird numbers. This could be true with $C=3$. We must have $C>2$ since $\sigma(70)=144$ but $70$ is not the distinct sum of integers from $\{1,2,5,7,10,14,35\}$. Erdos suggested that as $C\to \infty$ only divisors at most $\epsilon n$ need to be used, where $\epsilo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #827
problems.statement_preview: Let $n_k$ be minimal such that if $n_k$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in general position then there exists a subset of $k$ points such that all $\binom{k}{3}$ triples determine circles of different radii. Determine $n_k$.
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er75h} Erdos asks whether $n_k$ exists. In \cite{Er78c} he gave a simple argument which proves that it does, and in fact $ n_k \leq k+2\binom{k-1}{2}\binom{k-1}{3}, $ but this argument is incorrect, as explained by Martinez and Rold\'{a}n-Pensado \cite{MaRo15}. Martinez and Rold\'{a}n-Pensado give a corrected argument that proves $n_k\ll k^9$. References [Er75h] Erdos…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #828
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that, for any $a\in\mathbb{Z}$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that $ \phi(n) \mid n+a? $
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Graham. Lehmer has conjectured that $\phi(n)\mid n-1$ if and only if $n$ is prime. It is an easy exercise to show that $\phi(n) \mid n$ if and only if $n=2^a3^b$. This is discussed in problem B37 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.references_preview: Mordell proved that $ \limsup_{n\to \infty} 1_A\ast 1_A(n)=\infty $ and Mahler \cite{Ma35b} proved $ 1_A\ast 1_A(n) \gg (\log n)^{1/4} $ for infinitely many $n$. Stewart \cite{St08} improved this to $ 1_A\ast 1_A(n) \gg (\log n)^{11/13}. $ References [Ma35b] Mahler, Kurt, On the Lattice Points on Curves of Genus 1. Proc. London Math. Soc. (2) (1935), 431-466. problems.references_preview: For example $220$ and $284$. Erdos \cite{Er55b} proved that $A(x)=o(x)$, and Pomerance \cite{Po81} improved this to $ A(x) \leq x \exp(-(\log x)^{1/3}) $ and later \cite{Po15} to $ A(x) \leq x \exp(-(\tfrac{1}{2}+o(1))(\log x\log\log x)^{1/2}). $ This is problem B4 in Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. problems.title: Erdos Problem #850
problems.statement_preview: Can there exist two distinct integers $x$ and $y$ such that $x,y$ have the same prime factors, $x+1,y+1$ have the same prime factors, and $x+2,y+2$ also have the same prime factors?
problems.references_preview: This is sometimes known as the Erdos-Woods conjecture. For just $x,y$ and $x+1,y+1$ one can take $ x=2(2^r-1) $ and $ y = x(x+2). $ Erdos also asked whether there are any other examples. Makowski \cite{Ma68} observed that $x=75$ and $y=1215$ is another example, since $ 75 = 3\cdot 5^2 \textrm{ and }1215 = 3^5\cdot 5 $ while $ 76 = 2^2\cdot 19\textrm{ and }1216 = 2^6\cdot 19. $…

problems.references_preview: Erdos and Van Lint proved that $ H(n)-n^{3/2-o(1)}<G(n)<H(n) $ and $ \frac{H(n)-G(n)}{n}\to \infty. $ They proved that $G(n)>H(n)-n^{1+o(1)}$ assuming 'plausible (but hopeless) assumptions about the distribution of primes'. They also prove the second claim when $k=2$. See also [878].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #881
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset\mathbb{N}$ be an additive basis of order $k$ which is minimal, in the sense that if $B\subset A$ is any infinite set then $A\backslash B$ is not a basis of order $k$. Must there exist an infinite $B\subset A$ such that $A\backslash B$ is a basis of order $k+1$?", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #883
problems.statement_preview: 1. Under the threshold `|A| > floor(n/2)+floor(n/3)-floor(n/6)`, must `G(A)` contain all odd cycles of length `<= n/3 + 1`? 2. For every fixed `ell>=1`, under the same threshold and large `n`, must `G(A)` contain a complete `(1,ell,ell)` tripartite graph?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and S\'{a}rk\H{o}zy \cite{ErSa97}, who prove that if $ \lvert A\rvert >\lfloor\tfrac{n}{2}\rfloor+\lfloor\tfrac{n}{3}\rfloor-\lfloor\tfrac{n}{6}\rfloor $ then $G(A)$ contains all odd cycles of length $\leq cn$ for some constant $c>0$. This threshold is the best possible, since one could take $A$ to be the set of $m\leq n$ which are divisible by either $2$ or…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #884
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that, for any $n$, if $d_1<\cdots <d_t$ are the divisors of $n$, then $ \sum_{1\leq i<j\leq t}\frac{1}{d_j-d_i} \ll 1+\sum_{1\leq i<t}\frac{1}{d_{i+1}-d_i}, $ where the implied constant is absolute?", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #885
problems.statement_preview: For integer $n\geq 1$ we define the factor difference set of $n$ by $ D(n) = \{\lvert a-b\rvert : n=ab\}. $ Is it true that, for every $k\geq 1$, there exist integers $N_1<\cdots<N_k$ such that $ \lvert \cap_i D(N_i)\rvert \geq k? $
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Rosenfeld \cite{ErRo97}, who proved this is true for $k=2$. Jim\'{e}nez-Urroz \cite{Ji99} proved this for $k=3$ and Bremner \cite{Br19} proved this for $k=4$. References [Br19] Bremner, Andrew, On a problem of Erdos related to common factor differences. Int. J. Number Theory (2019), 1059--1068. [ErRo97] Erdos, Paul and Rosenfeld, Moshe, The factor-diffe…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #886
problems.statement_preview: Let $\epsilon>0$. Is it true that, for all large $n$, the number of divisors of $n$ in $(n^{1/2},n^{1/2}+n^{1/2-\epsilon})$ is $O_\epsilon(1)$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos attributes this conjecture to Ruzsa. Erdos and Rosenfeld \cite{ErRo97} proved that there are infinitely many $n$ such that there are four divisors of $n$ in $(n^{1/2},n^{1/2}+n^{1/4})$. See also [887]. References [ErRo97] Erdos, Paul and Rosenfeld, Moshe, The factor-difference set of integers. Acta Arith. (1997), 353--359.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #887
problems.statement_preview: Is there an absolute constant $K$ such that, for every $C>0$, if $n$ is sufficiently large then $n$ has at most $K$ divisors in $(n^{1/2},n^{1/2}+C n^{1/4})$.
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Rosenfeld \cite{ErRo97}, who proved that there are infinitely many $n$ with $4$ divisors in $(n^{1/2},n^{1/2}+n^{1/4})$, and ask whether $4$ is best possible here. References [ErRo97] Erdos, Paul and Rosenfeld, Moshe, The factor-difference set of integers. Acta Arith. (1997), 353--359. ", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #888

problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos, S\'{a}rk"{o}zy, and S\'{o}s. Erdos claims that S\'{a}rk"{o}zy proved that $\lvert A\rvert =o(n)$ (a proof of this bound is provided by Tao in the comments). The primes show that $\lvert A\rvert \gg n/\log n$ is possible. Cambie and Weisenberg have noted in the comments that the set of semiprimes also works, showing $ (1+o(1))\frac{\log\log n}{\log n}n \leq…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #889
problems.statement_preview: For $k\geq 0$ and $n\geq 1$ let $v(n,k)$ count the prime factors of $n+k$ which do not divide $n+i$ for $0\leq i<k$. Equivalently, $v(n,k)$ counts the number of prime factors of $n+k$ which are $>k$. Is it true that $ v_0(n)=\max_{k\geq 0}v(n,k)\to \infty $ as $n\to \infty$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Selfridge \cite{ErSe67}, who could only show that $v_0(n)\geq 2$ for $n\geq 17$. More generally, they conjecture that $ v_l(n)=\max_{k\geq l}v(n,k)\to \infty $ as $n\to \infty$, for every fixed $l$, but could not even prove that $v_1(n)\geq 2$ for all large $n$. This is problem B27 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [ErSe67] Erdos, P. and Selfr…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #890
problems.statement_preview: For fixed `k`, study `S_k(n)=sum_{i=0}^{k-1} omega(n+i)` and ask: 1. whether `liminf S_k(n) <= k+pi(k)`, 2. whether `limsup S_k(n) * loglog n / log n = 1`.
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Selfridge \cite{ErSe67}, who observe that $ \liminf_{n\to \infty}\sum_{0\leq i<k}\omega(n+i)\geq k+\pi(k)-1 $ for every $k$. This follows from P\'{o}lya's theorem that the set of $k$-smooth integers has unbounded gaps - indeed, $n(n+1)\cdots (n+k-1)$ is divisible by all primes $\leq k$ and, provided $n$ is large, all but at most one of $n,n+1,\ldots,n+k…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #891
problems.statement_preview: For primes `2=p_1<p_2<...` and fixed `k>=2`, must every sufficiently large interval `[n, n+p_1...p_k)` contain a number with more than `k` prime factors?
problems.established_preview: Background records a nearby positive variant (Schin

This is unknown even for $k=2$ - that is, is it true that in every interval of $6$ (sufficiently large) consecutive integers there must exist one with at least $3$ prime factors? Weise…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #893
problems.statement_preview: If $\tau(n)$ counts the divisors of $n$ then let $ f(n)=\sum_{1\leq k\leq n}\tau(2^k-1). $ Does $f(2n)/f(n)$ tend to a limit?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er98} says that 'probably there is no simple asymptotic formula for $f(n)$ since $f(n)$ increases too fast'. Kova\v{c} and Luca \cite{KoLu25} (building on a heuristic independently found by Cambie (personal communication)) have shown that there is no finite limit, in that $ \limsup_{n\to \infty}\frac{f(2n)}{f(n)}=\infty, $ and provide both theoretical and numerical…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #896
problems.statement_preview: For A,B subset {1,...,N}, let F(A,B) count products m=ab that have exactly one representation with a in A, b in B. Maximi
problems.established_preview: Background/comments give two-sided bounds: (1+o(1))N^2/log N <= max F(A,B) and max F(A,B) << N^2 / ((log N)^delta (log log N)^{3/2}) for explicit delta>0.
problems.references_preview: In the comments van Doorn proves $ (1+o(1))\frac{N^2}{\log N}\leq \max_{A,B}F(A,B) \ll \frac{N^2}{(\log N)^\delta(\log\log N)^{3/2}} $ where $\delta=1-\frac{1+\log\log 2}{\log 2}\approx 0.086$. See also [490].", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #901
problems.statement_preview: Let $m(n)$ be minimal such that there is an $n$-uniform hypergraph with $m(n)$ edges which is $3$-chromatic. Estimate $m(n)$.
problems.references_preview: In other words, the hypergraph does not have Property B. Property B means that there is a set $S$ which intersects all edges and yet does not contain any edge. It is known that $m(2)=3$, $m(3)=7$, and $m(4)=23$. Erdos proved $ 2^n \ll m(n) \ll n^2 2^n $ (the lower bound in \cite{Er63b} and the upper bound in \cite{Er64e}). Erdos conjectured that $m(n)/2^n\to \infty$, which was…

Congr. Numer. (1982), 25-45.", "difficulty": …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #917
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 4$ and $f_k(n)$ be the largest number of edges in a graph on $n$ vertices which has chromatic number $k$ and is critical (i.e. deleting any edge reduces the chromatic number). Is it true that $ f_k(n) \gg_k n^2? $ Is it true that $ f_6(n)\sim n^2/4? $ More generally, is it true that, for $k\geq 6$, $ f_k(n) \sim \frac{1}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{\lfloor k/3\rfloor}\right)…
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er93} wrote 'I learned of this definition from Dirac in 1949 and immediately asked whether $f_k(n)=o(n^2)$. To my great surprise Dirac constructed a $6$ critical graph on $n$ vertices with more than $\frac{n^2}{4}$ edges.' In fact Dirac \cite{Di52} proved $ f_6(4n+2) \geq 4n^2+8n+3, $ as witnessed by taking two disjoint copies of $C_{2n+1}$ and adding all edges bet…

problems.references_preview: This question was inspired by a theorem of Babai, that if $G$ is a graph on a well-ordered set with chromatic number $\geq \aleph_0$ there is a subgraph on vertices with order-type $\omega$ with chromatic number $\aleph_0$. problems.title: Erdos Problem #920
problems.statement_preview: Let $f_k(n)$ be the maximum possible chromatic number of a graph with $n$ vertices which contains no $K_k$. Is it true that, for $k\geq 4$, $ f_k(n) \gg \frac{n^{1-\frac{1}{k-1}}}{(\log n)^{c_k}} $ for some constant $c_k>0$?
problems.references_preview: Graver and Yackel \cite{GrYa68} proved that $ f_k(n) \ll \left(n\frac{\log\log n}{\log n}\right)^{1-\frac{1}{k-1}}. $ It is known that $f_3(n)\asymp (n/\log n)^{1/2}$ (see [1104]). The lower bound $R(4,m) \gg m^3/(\log m)^4$ of Mattheus and Verstraete \cite{MaVe23} (see [166]) implies $ f_4(n) \gg \frac{n^{2/3}}{(\log n)^{4/3}}. $ A positive answer to this question would follo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #928
problems.statement_preview: Let $\alpha,\beta\in (0,1)$ and let $P(n)$ denote the largest prime divisor of $n$. Does the density of integers $n$ such that $P(n)<n^{\alpha}$ and $P(n+1)<(n+1)^\beta$ exist?
problems.references_preview: Dickman \cite{Di30} showed the density of smooth $n$, with largest prime factor $<n^\alpha$, is $\rho(1/\alpha)$ where $\rho$ is the Dickman function. Erdos also asked whether infinitely many such $n$ even exist, but Meza has observed that this follows immediately from Schinzel's result \cite{Sc67b} that for infinitely many $n$ the largest prime factor of $n(n+1)$ is at most $…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #929
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 2$ be large and let $S(k)$ be the minimal $x$ such that there is a positive density set of $n$ where $ n+1,n+2,\ldots,n+k $ are all divisible by primes $\leq x$. Estimate $S(k)$ - in particular, is it true that $S(k)\geq k^{1-o(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: It follows from Rosser's sieve that $S(k)> k^{1/2-o(1)}$. It is trivial that $S(k)\leq k+1$ since, for example, one can take $n\equiv 1\pmod{(k+1)!}$. The best bound on large gaps between primes due to Ford, Green, Konyagin, Maynard, and Tao \cite{FGKMT18} (see [4]) implies $ S(k) \ll k \frac{\log\log\log k}{\log\log k\log\log\log\log k}. $ References [FGKMT18] Ford, Kevin and…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #930
problems.statement_preview: For each `r`, ask if there is `k` so that for any `r` disjoint intervals `I_1,...,I_r` of consecutive integers, each of length at least `k`, the product `prod_i prod_{m in I_i} m` is not a perfect power.
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Selfridge \cite{ErSe75} proved that the product of consecutive integers is never a power (establishing the case $r=1$). The condition that the intervals be large in terms of $r$ is necessary for $r=2$ - see the constructions in [363]. See also [363] for the case of squares. References [ErSe75] Erdos, P. and Selfridge, J. L., The product of consecutive integers is nev…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #931
problems.statement_preview: Let $k_1\geq k_2\geq 3$. Are there only finitely many $n_2\geq n_1+k_1$ such that $ \prod_{1\leq i\leq k_1}(n_1+i)\textrm{ and }\prod_{1\leq j\leq k_2}(n_2+j) $ have the same prime factors?
problems.references_preview: Tijdeman gave the example $ 19,20,21,22\textrm{ and }54,55,56,57. $ Erdos \cite{Er76d} was unsure of this conjecture, and thought perhaps if the two products have the same prime factors then $n_2>2(n_1+k_1)$. It is not clear but it is possible that he meant to ask this question also permitting finitely many counterexamples. Indeed, without this caveat it is false - AlphaProof …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #932
problems.statement_preview: Conjecture: for infinitely many r, there are at least two integers n in (p_r,p_{r+1}) whose prime factors are all < p_{r+1}-p_r.
problems.established_preview: Background records a one-sided rarity result: Erdos proved the set of r for which at least one such n exists has density 0.
problems.references_preview: Erdos thought this was true but that there are very few such $r$. He could show that the density of $r$ such that at least one such $n$ exist is $0$. This problem has been formalised in Lean as part of the Google DeepMind Formal Conjectures project. ", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #939
problems.statement_preview: Let $r\geq 2$. An $r$-powerful number $n$ is one such that if $p\mid n$ then $p^r\mid n$. If $r\geq 4$ then can the sum of $r-2$ coprime $r$-powerful numbers ever be itself $r$-powerful? Are there at most finitely many such solutions? Are there infinitely many triples of coprime $3$-powerful numbers $a,b,c$ such that $a+b=c$?
problems.references_preview: The answer to the third question is yes: Nitaj \cite{Ni95} has proved that there are infinitely many triples of coprime $3$-powerful numbers $a,b,c$ such that $a+b=c$, such as $ 2^3\cdot 3^5\cdot 73^3+271^3 = 919^3. $ In Nitaj's construction at least two of $a,b,c$ are perfect cubes. Cohn \cite{Co98} constructed infinitely many such triples, none of which are perfect cubes. An…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #940

Are there infinitely many integers which are not the sum of at most $r$ many $r$-powerful numbers? Does the set of integers which are the sum of at most $r$ $r$-powerful numbers have density $0$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er76d} claims that the claim that the set has density $0$ is 'easy' for $r=2$ (a potential 'easy argument' is given in the comments by Tao; this was first proved in the literature by Baker and Br"{u}dern \cite{BaBr94}). problems.title: Erdos Problem #942

problems.references_preview: Erdos writes it is not hard to prove that $\limsup h(n)=\infty$, and that the density $\delta_l$ of integers for which $h(n)=l$ exists and $\sum \delta_l=1$. A proof that $h(n)$ is unbounded is provided by van Doorn in the comments. De Koninck and Luca \cite{DeLu04} have proved, for infinitely many $n$, $ h(n) \gg \left(\frac{\log n}{\log\log n}\right)^{1/3}. $ They also give …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #944
problems.statement_preview: A critical vertex, edge, or set of edges, is one whose deletion lowers the chromatic number. Let $k\geq 4$ and $r\geq 1$. Must there exist a graph $G$ with chromatic number $k$ such that every vertex is critical, yet every critical set of edges has size $>r$?
problems.references_preview: A graph $G$ with chromatic number $k$ in which every vertex is critical is called $k$-vertex-critical. This was conjectured by Dirac in 1970 for $k\geq 4$ and $r=1$. Dirac's conjecture was proved, for $k=5$, by Brown \cite{Br92}. Lattanzio \cite{La02} proved there exist such graphs for all $k$ such that $k-1$ is not prime. Independently, Jensen \cite{Je02} gave an alternative …

Estimate $F(x)$. Beker has improved the upper bound to $ F(x) \ll \exp\left(O\left((\log x…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #948
problems.statement_preview: Seek k and growth control a_n<f(n) infinitely often such that finite-sums set of (a_n) misses at least one color in every k-coloring variant.
problems.established_preview: Background gives a negative result for a stronger version: Galvin constructed a 2-coloring showing one cannot always force monochromatic finite-sums behavior in the originally stronger formulation.
problems.references_preview: Erdos initially asked whether this is possible with the set being monochromatic, but Galvin showed that this is not always possible, considering the two colouring where, writing $n=2^km$ with $m$ odd, we colour $n$ red if $m\geq F(k)$ and blue if $m<F(k)$ (for some sufficiently quickly growing $F$). This is open even in the case of $\aleph_0$-many colours. This is asking about…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #950
problems.statement_preview: For f(n)=sum_{p<n}1/(n-p), ask about liminf, limsup, and growth bounds.
problems.established_preview: Background provides substantial information: - average and second moment: sum_{n<x} f(n) ~ x and sum_{n<x} f(n)^2 ~ x; - certain short-interval prime lower bounds imply liminf f(n)>0; - additional strong prime-distribution hypotheses would imply f(n)<<log log log n.
problems.references_preview: This function was considered by de Bruijn, Erdos, and Tur\'{a}n, who showed that $ \sum_{n<x}f(n)\sim \sum_{n<x}f(n)^2\sim x. $ The existence of some $c>0$ such that there are $\gg n^c/\log n$ many primes in $[n,n+n^c]$ implies that $\liminf f(n)>0$. Erdos writes that a 'weaker conjecture which is perhaps not quite inaccessible' is that, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $x$ is suffi…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #953

The trivial upper bound is $O(r)$. Kovac has observed that S\'{a}rk"{o}zy's lower bound in [466] can be adapted to give a lower bound of $\gg r^{0.26}$ for this problem. See also [465] for a similar problem (concerning upper bound…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #954
problems.statement_preview: For Rosen's recursively defined sequence, let R(x)=# {(i,j): i<=j, a_i+a_j<=x }. Ask whether R(x)=x+O(x^{1/4+o(1)}).
problems.established_preview: Background gives the built-in lower control R(x)>=x by construction.
problems.references_preview: This sequence was constructed by Rosen. Note that the number of solutions to $a_i+a_j\leq x$ is always at least $x$ by construction. Erdos and Rosen could not even prove whether the number of solutions to $a_i+a_j\leq x$ satisfies is at most $(1+o(1))x$. The sequence begins $ 1,3,5,9,13,17,24,31,38,45,\ldots. $ ", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #955
problems.statement_preview: Let $ s(n)=\sigma(n)-n=\sum_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}}d $ be the sum of proper divisors function. If $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ has density $0$ then $s^{-1}(A)$ must also have density $0$.
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Erdos, Granville, Pomerance, and Spiro \cite{EGPS90}. It is possible for $s(A)$ to have positive density even if $A$ has zero density (for example taking $A$ to be the product of two distinct primes). problems.title: Erdos Problem #959
problems.statement_preview: Let $A\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a set of size $n$ and let $\{d_1,\ldots,d_k\}$ be the set of distinct distances determined by $A$. Let $f(d)$ be the number of times the distance $d$ is determined, and suppose the $d_i$ are ordered such that $ f(d_1)\geq f(d_2)\geq \cdots \geq f(d_k). $ Estimate $ \max (f(d_1)-f(d_2)), $ where the maximum is taken over all $A$ of size $n$.

problems.title: Erdos Problem #968

problems.title: Erdos Problem #969
problems.statement_preview: Let $Q(x)$ count the number of squarefree integers in $[1,x]$. Determine the order of magnitude in the error term in the asymptotic $ Q(x)=\frac{6}{\pi^2}x+E(x). $
problems.references_preview: It is elementary to prove $E(x)\ll x^{1/2}$, and the prime number theorem implies $o(x^{1/2})$. The best known unconditional upper bound is of the shape $x^{1/2-o(1)}$, due to Walfisz \cite{Wa63}. Evelyn and Linfoot \cite{EvLi31} proved that $ E(x) \gg x^{1/4}, $ and this is likely the true order of magnitude. The Riemann Hypothesis would follow from $E(x)\ll x^{1/4}$. The tru…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #970
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(k)$ be Jacobsthal's function, defined to as the minimal $m$ such that, if $n$ has at most $k$ prime factors, then in any set of $m$ consecutive integers there exists an integer coprime to $n$. Determine the order of magnitude of $h(k)$. In particular, is it true that $ h(k) \ll k^2? $
problems.references_preview: That $h(k)\ll k^2$ is a conjecture of Jacobsthal. Iwaniec \cite{Iw78} proved $ h(k) \ll (k\log k)^2. $ The best lower bound known is $ h(k) \gg \frac{(\log k)(\log\log\log k)}{(\log\log k)^2}k, $ due to Ford, Green, Konyagin, Maynard, and Tao \cite{FGKMT18}. This is a more general form of the function considered in [687]. References [FGKMT18] Ford, Kevin and Green, Ben and Kon…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #971
problems.statement_preview: Let $p(a,d)$ be the least prime congruent to $a\pmod{d}$. Does there exist a constant $c>0$ such that, for all large $d$, $ p(a,d) > (1+c)\phi(d)\log d $ for $\gg \phi(d)$ many values of $a$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er49c} could prove this is true for an infinite sequence of $d$. He also proved that, for any $\epsilon>0$, $ p(a,d)< \epsilon \phi(d)\log d $ for $\gg_\epsilon \phi(d)$ many values of $a$. References [Er49c] Erdos, P., On some applications of {B}run's method. problems.title: Erdos Problem #997
problems.statement_preview: Call $x_1,x_2,\ldots \in (0,1)$ well-distributed if, for every $\epsilon>0$, if $k$ is sufficiently large then, for all $n>0$ and intervals $I\subseteq [0,1]$, $ \lvert \# \{ n<m\leq n+k : x_m\in I\} - \lvert I\rvert k\rvert < \epsilon k. $ Is it true that, for every $\alpha$, the sequence $\{ \alpha p_n\}$ is not well-distributed, if $p_n$ is the sequence of primes?
problems.references_preview: The notion of a well-distributed sequence was introduced by Hlawka and Petersen \cite{Hl55}. Erdos proved that, if $n_k$ is a lacunary sequence, then the sequence $\{ \alpha n_k\}$ is not well-distributed for almost all $\alpha$. He also claimed in \cite{Er64b} to have proved that there exists an irrational $\alpha$ for which $\{\alpha p_n\}$ is not well-distributed. He later …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1002
problems.statement_preview: For `f(alpha,n)=(1/log n) sum_{k<=n} (1/2-{alpha k})`, ask existence of an asymptotic distribution over `alpha in (0,1)`.
problems.references_preview: Kesten \cite{Ke60} proved that if $ f(\alpha,\beta,n)=\frac{1}{\log n}\sum_{1\leq k\leq n}(\tfrac{1}{2}-\{\beta+\alpha k\}) $ then $f(\alpha,\beta,n)$ has asymptotic distribution function $ g(c)=\frac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\rho c}\frac{1}{1+t^2}\mathrm{d}t, $ where $\rho>0$ is an explicit constant. References [Ke60] Kesten, Harry, Uniform distribution {${\rm mod}\,1$}. Ann. o…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1003
problems.statement_preview: Are there infinitely many solutions to $\phi(n)=\phi(n+1)$, where $\phi$ is the Euler totient function?
problems.established_preview: No proof of infinitude or counterexample; finite data supports continued occurrence.
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er85e} says that, presumably, for every $k\geq 1$ the equation $ \phi(n)=\phi(n+1)=\cdots=\phi(n+k) $ has infinitely many solutions. Erdos, Pomerance, and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy \cite{EPS87} proved that the number of $n\leq x$ with $\phi(n)=\phi(n+1)$ is at most $ \frac{x}{\exp((\log x)^{1/3})}. $ See [946] for the analogous question with the divisor function. References […

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1004
problems.statement_preview: Let $c>0$. If $x$ is sufficiently large then does there exist $n\leq x$ such that the values of $\phi(n+k)$ are all distinct for $1\leq k\leq (\log x)^c$, where $\phi$ is the Euler totient function?
problems.established_preview: Large finite distinct runs found; full asymptotic theorem remains open.
problems.references_preview: Erdos, Pomerenace, and S\'{a}rk"{o}zy \cite{EPS87} proved that if $\phi(n+k)$ are all distinct for $1\leq k\leq K$ then $ K \leq \frac{n}{\exp(c(\log n)^{1/3})} $ for some constant $c>0$. See [945] for the analogous problem with the divisor function. References [EPS87] Erdos, Paul and Pomerance, Carl and S\'ark"ozy, Andr\'as, On locally repeated values of certain arithmetic fu…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1005
problems.statement_preview: Let $\frac{a_1}{b_1},\frac{a_2}{b_2},\ldots$ be the Farey fractions of order $n\geq 4$. Let $f(n)$ be the largest integer such that if $1\leq k<l\leq k+f(n)$ then $\frac{a_k}{b_k}$ and $\frac{a_l}{b_l}$ are similarly ordered - in other words, $ (a_k-a_l)(b_k-b_l)\geq 0. $ Estimate $f(n)$ - in particular, is there a constant $c>0$ such that $f(n)=(c+o(1))n$ for all large $n$?
problems.references_preview: The function $f(n)$ was first considered by Mayer \cite{Ma42}, who proved $f(n)\to \infty$ as $n\to \infty$. Erdos \cite{Er43} proved $f(n)\gg n$. van Doorn \cite{vD25b} has proved that $ \left(\frac{1}{12}-o(1)\right)n\leq f(n) \leq \frac{1}{4}n+O(1), $ and conjectures that the upper bound is optimal. References [Er43] Erdos, P., A note on {F}arey series. Quart. J. Math. Oxfo…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1011
problems.statement_preview: Let $f_r(n)$ be minimal such that every graph on $n$ vertices with $\geq f_r(n)$ edges and chromatic number $\geq r$ contains a triangle. Determine $f_r(n)$.
problems.references_preview: Tur\'{a}n's theorem implies $f_2(n)=\lfloor n^2/4\rfloor+1$. Erdos and Gallai \cite{Er62d} proved $f_3(n)=\lfloor \frac{1}{4}(n-1)^2\rfloor+2$. Simonovits showed in his PhD thesis (see the discussion on p. 358 of \cite{Si74}) that $ f_r(n)=\frac{n^2}{4}-\frac{g(r)}{2}{n}+O(1), $ where $g(r)$ is the largest $m$ such that, for any triangle-free graph with chromatic number $\geq …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1013
problems.statement_preview: For `h_3(k)` (minimum order of a triangle-free graph with chromatic number `k`), find asymptotics and prove ratio smoothness `h_3(k+1)/h_3(k)->1`.
problems.references_preview: The function $h_3(k)$ is dual to the function $f(n)$ considered in [1104], in that $h_3(k)= n$ if and only if $n$ is minimal such that $f(n)=k$. Graver and Yackel \cite{GrYa68} proved $ h_3(k)\gg \frac{\log k}{\log\log k}k^2. $ The bounds for $f(n)$ from [1104] imply $ \left(\frac{1}{2}-o(1)\right)k^2\log k\leq h_3(k) \leq (1+o(1))k^2\log k. $ See also [920] for a generalisati…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1014
problems.statement_preview: For fixed k>=3, prove R(k,l+1)/R(k,l)->1 as l->infinity.
problems.established_preview: - no proof or counterexample obtained in this attempt.
problems.references_preview: This is open even for $k=3$. See also [544] for other behaviour of $R(3,k)$, and [1030] for the diagonal version of this question.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1016
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(n)$ be minimal such that there is a graph on $n$ vertices with $n+h(n)$ edges which contains a cycle on $k$ vertices, for all $3\leq k\leq n$. Estimate $h(n)$. In particular, is it true that $ h(n) \geq \log_2n+\log_*n-O(1), $ where $\log_*n$ is the iterated logarithmic function?
problems.references_preview: Such graphs are called pancyclic. A problem of Bondy \cite{Bo71}, who claimed a proof (without details) of $ \log_2(n-1)-1\leq h(n) \leq \log_2n+\log_*n+O(1). $ Erdos \cite{Er71} believed the upper bound is closer to the truth, but could not even prove $h(n)-\log_2n\to \infty$. A proof of the above lower bound is provided by Griffin \cite{Gr13}. The first published proof of th…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1017
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n,k)$ be such that every graph on $n$ vertices and $k$ edges can be partitioned into at most $f(n,k)$ edge-disjoint complete graphs. Estimate $f(n,k)$ for $k>n^2/4$.
problems.references_preview: The function $f(n,k)$ is sometimes called the clique partition number. Erdos, Goodman, and P\'{o}sa \cite{EGP66} proved that $f(n,k)\leq n^2/4$ for all $k$ (and in fact the complete graphs can be taken to be edges and triangles), which is best possible in general, as witnessed for example by a complete bipartite graph. In \cite{Er71} Erd\H{o} asks vaguely whether this result c…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1021
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that, for every $k\geq 3$, there is a constant $c_k>0$ such that $ \mathrm{ex}(n,G_k) \ll n^{3/2-c_k}, $ where $G_k$ is the bipartite graph between $\{y_1,\ldots,y_k\}$ and $\{z_1,\ldots,z_{\binom{k}{2}}\}$, with each $z_j$ joined to a unique pair of $y_i$?
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Erdos and Simonovits, who proved (in unpublished work) that in such a result one must have $c_k\to 0$ as $k\to \infty$. Erdos \cite{Er71} could not even prove whether $\mathrm{ex}(n,G_k)=o(n^{3/2})$. When $k=3$ the graph $G_3$ is the $6$-cycle $C_6$, for which Erdos \cite{Er64c} and Bondy and Simonovits \cite{BoSi74} proved $\mathrm{ex}(n,C_6)\ll n^{7/6}$ (see …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1022
problems.statement_preview: Find `c_t` (with `c_t->infinity`) such that local subset-density condition `# {A in F: A subset X} < c_t |X|` for all `X` forces property B.
problems.references_preview: Erdos originally conjectured, in this language, that $c_2=1$, which was proved by Lov\'{a}sz \cite{Lo68}. References [Lo68] Lov\'{a}sz, L., On covering of graphs. Theory of Graphs (Proc. Colloq., Tihany, 1966) (1968), 231-236.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1029
problems.statement_preview: If $R(k)$ is the Ramsey number for $K_k$, the minimal $n$ such that every $2$-colouring of the edges of $K_n$ contains a monochromatic copy of $K_k$, then $ \frac{R(k)}{k2^{k/2}}\to \infty. $
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er93} Erdos offers \$100 for a proof of this and \$1000 for a disproof, but says 'this last offer is to some extent phoney: I am sure that [this] is true (but I have been wrong before).' Erdos and Szekeres \cite{ErSz35} proved $ k2^{k/2} \ll R(k) \leq \binom{2k-1}{k-1}. $ One of the first applications of the probabilistic method pioneered by Erdos gives $ R(k) \geq (1…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1030
problems.statement_preview: If $R(k,l)$ is the Ramsey number then prove the existence of some $c>0$ such that $ \lim_k \frac{R(k+1,k)}{R(k,k)}> 1+c. $
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and S\'{o}s, who could not even prove whether $R(k+1,k)-R(k,k)>k^c$ for any $c>1$. It is trivial that $R(k+1,k)-R(k,k)\geq k-2$. Burr, Erdos, Faudree, and Schelp \cite{BEFS89} proved $ R(k+1,k)-R(k,k)\geq 2k-5. $ See also [544] for a similar question concerning $R(3,k)$, and [1014] for the general off-diagonal case. References [BEFS89] Burr, S. A. and Erdos,…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1032
problems.statement_preview: We say that a graph is $4$-chromatic critical if it has chromatic number $4$, and removing any edge decreases the chromatic number to $3$. Is there, for arbitrarily large $n$, a $4$-chromatic critical graph on $n$ vertices with minimum degree $\gg n$?
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er93} Erdos said he asked this 'more than 20 years ago'. Dirac gave an example of a $6$-chromatic critical graph with minimum degree $>n/2$. This problem is also open for $5$-chromatic critical graphs. Simonovits \cite{Si72} and Toft \cite{To72} independently constructed $4$-chromatic critical graphs with minimum degree $\gg n^{1/3}$. Toft conjectured that a $4$-chrom…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1033
problems.statement_preview: Let $h(n)$ be such that every graph on $n$ vertices with $>n^2/4$ many edges contains a triangle whose vertices have degrees summing to at least $h(n)$. Estimate $h(n)$. In particular, is it true that $ h(n)\geq (2(\sqrt{3}-1)-o(1))n? $
problems.references_preview: Erdos and Laskar \cite{ErLa85} proved $ 2(\sqrt{3}-1)n \geq h(n) \geq (1+c)n $ for some $c>0$. The lower bound was improved to $\frac{21}{16}n$ by Fan \cite{Fa88}. References [ErLa85] Erdos, Paul and Laskar, Renu, A note on the size of a chordal subgraph. Congr. Numer. (1985), 81--86. [Fa88] Fan, Genghua, Degree sum for a triangle in a graph. J. Graph Theory (1988), 249--263."…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1035
problems.statement_preview: Is there a constant $c>0$ such that every graph on $2^n$ vertices with minimum degree $>(1-c)2^n$ contains the $n$-dimensional hypercube $Q_n$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er93} says 'if the conjecture is false, two related problems could be asked': {UL} {LI}Determine or estimate the smallest $m>2^n$ such that every graph on $m$ vertices with minimum degree $>(1-c)2^n$ contains a $Q_n$, and {/LI} {LI}For which $u_n$ is it true that every graph on $2^n$ vertices with minimum degree $>2^n-u_n$ contains a $Q_n$.{/LI} {/UL} See also [576…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1038
problems.statement_preview: Determine infimum/supremum of `|{x:|f(x)|<1}|` over monic real polynomials with all real roots in `[-1,1]`.
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos, Herzog, and Piranian \cite{EHP58}, who proved that the measure of the set in question is always at most $2\sqrt{2}$ under the assumption that all the roots are in $\{-1,1\}$, and conjecture this is the best possible upper bound. They also note that the infimum of the set in question is less than $2$, as witnessed by $f(x)=(x+1)(x-1)^m$ for $m\geq 3$. They f…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1039
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(z)=\prod_{i=1}^n(z-z_i)\in \mathbb{C}[z]$ with $\lvert z_i\rvert \leq 1$ for all $i$. Let $\rho(f)$ be the radius of the largest disc which is contained in $\{z: \lvert f(z)\rvert< 1\}$. Determine the behaviour of $\rho(f)$. In particular, is it always true that $\rho(f)\gg 1/n$?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos, Herzog, and Piranian, who note that $f(z)=z^n-1$ has $\rho(f) \leq \frac{\pi/2}{n}$. Pommerenke \cite{Po61} proved that $ \rho(f) \geq \frac{1}{2en^2}. $ Krishnapur, Lundberg, and Ramachandran \cite{KLR25} proved $ \rho(f) \gg \frac{1}{n\sqrt{\log n}}. $ References [KLR25] M. Krishnapur, E. Lundberg, and K. Ramachandran, On the area of polynomial lemniscate…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1040
problems.statement_preview: For closed infinite `F subset C`, is `mu(F)` determined by transfinite diameter? In particular, if diameter is `>=1`, must `mu(F)=0`?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos, Herzog, and Piranian \cite{EHP58}, who show that the answer is yes if $F$ is a line segment or disc, and that if the transfinite diameter is $<1$ then $\{ z: \lvert f(z)\rvert < 1\}$ always contains a disc of radius $\gg_F 1$. Erdos and Netanyahu \cite{ErNe73} proved that if $F$ is also bounded and connected, with transfinite diameter $0<c<1$, then $\{ z: \…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1049
problems.statement_preview: For rational `t>1`, is `sum_{n>=1} 1/(t^n-1) = sum_{n>=1} tau(n)/t^n` irrational?
problems.established_preview: Background says Erdos proved irrationality when `t` is an integer `>=2`.
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Chowla. Erdos \cite{Er48} proved that this is true if $t\geq 2$ is an integer. References [Er48] Erdos, P., On arithmetical properties of Lambert series. J. Indian Math. Soc. (N.S.) (1948), 63-66.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1051
problems.statement_preview: Is it true that if $a_1<a_2<\cdots$ is a sequence of integers with $ \liminf a_n^{1/2^n}>1 $ then $ \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{1}{a_na_{n+1}} $ is irrational?
problems.references_preview: In \cite{Er88c} Erdos notes this is true if $a_n\to \infty$ 'rapidly'. References [Er88c] Erd"{o}s, P., On the irrationality of certain series: problems and results. New advances in transcendence theory (Durham, 1986) (1988), 102-109.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1052
problems.statement_preview: A unitary divisor of $n$ is $d\mid n$ such that $(d,n/d)=1$. A number $n\geq 1$ is a unitary perfect number if it is the sum of its unitary divisors (aside from $n$ itself). Are there only finite many unitary perfect numbers?
problems.references_preview: Guy \cite{Gu04} reports that Carlitz, Erdos, and Subbarao offer \$10 for settling this question, and that Subbarao offers 10 cents for each new example. There are no odd unitary perfect numbers. There are five known unitary perfect numbers (A002827 in the OEIS): $ 6, 60, 90, 87360, 146361946186458562560000. $ This is problem B3 in Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1053
problems.statement_preview: Call a number $k$-perfect if $\sigma(n)=kn$, where $\sigma(n)$ is the sum of the divisors of $n$. Must $k=o(\log\log n)$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos, as reported in problem B2 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. Guy further writes 'It has even been suggested that there may be only finitely many $k$-perfect numbers with $k\geq 3$.' The largest $k$ for which a $k$-perfect number has been found is $k=11$ - see this page for more information. These are known as multiply perfect numbers. When $k=2$ this is the …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1054
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ be the minimal integer $m$ such that $n$ is the sum of the $k$ smallest divisors of $m$ for some $k\geq 1$. Is it true that $f(n)=o(n)$? Or is this true only for almost all $n$, and $\limsup f(n)/n=\infty$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos reported in problem B2 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. The function $f(n)$ is undefined for $n=2$ and $n=5$, but is likely well-defined for all $n\geq 6$ (which would follow from a strong form of Goldbach's conjecture). The sequence of values of $f(n)$ is given by A167485 in the OEIS. See also [468]. The strong claim that $f(n)=o(n)$ was disproved by Tao i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1055
problems.statement_preview: A prime $p$ is in class $1$ if the only prime divisors of $p+1$ are $2$ or $3$. In general, a prime $p$ is in class $r$ if every prime factor of $p+1$ is in some class $\leq r-1$, with equality for at least one prime factor. Are there infinitely many primes in each class? If $p_r$ is the least prime in class $r$, then how does $p_r^{1/r}$ behave?
problems.references_preview: A classification due to Erdos and Selfridge. It is easy to prove that the number of primes $\leq n$ in class $r$ is at most $n^{o(1)}$. The sequence $p_r$ begins $2,13,37,73,1021$ (A005113 in the OEIS). Erdos thought $p_r^{1/r}\to \infty$, while Selfridge thought it quite likely to be bounded. A similar question can be asked replacing $p+1$ with $p-1$. This is problem A18 in G…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1056
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 2$. Does there exist a prime $p$ and consecutive intervals $I_1,\ldots,I_k$ such that $ \prod_{n\in I_i}n \equiv 1\pmod{p} $ for all $1\leq i\leq k$?
problems.references_preview: This is problem A15 in Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}, where he reports that in a letter in 1979 Erdos observed that $ 3\cdot 4\equiv 5\cdot 6\cdot 7\equiv 1\pmod{11}, $ establishing the case $k=2$. Makowski \cite{Ma83} found, for $k=3$, $ 2\cdot 3\cdot 4\cdot 5\equiv 6\cdot 7\cdot 8\cdot 9\cdot 10\cdot 11\equiv 12\cdot 13\cdot 14\cdot 15\equiv 1\pmod{17}. $ Noll and Simmons ask…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1057
problems.statement_preview: Let $C(x)$ count the number of Carmichael numbers in the interval $[1,x]$. Is it true that $C(x)=x^{1-o(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er56c} proved $ C(x) < x \exp\left(-c \frac{\log x\log\log\log x}{\log\log x}\right) $ for some constant $c>0$. Pomerance \cite{Po89} gave a heuristic suggesting that this is the true order of growth, and in fact $ C(x)= x \exp\left(-(1+o(1))\frac{\log x\log\log\log x}{\log\log x}\right). $ Alford, Granville, and Pomerance \cite{AGP94} proved that $C(x)\to \infty$,…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1059
problems.statement_preview: Are there infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p-k!$ is composite for each $k$ such that $1\leq k!<p$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos reported in problem A2 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. Examples are $p=101$ and $p=211$. Erdos suggested it may be easier to show that there are infinitely many $n$ such that, if $l!<n\leq (l+1)!$, then all the prime factors of $n$ are $>l$, and all the numbers $n-k!$ are composite for $1\leq k\leq l$. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems i…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1060
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ count the number of solutions to $k\sigma(k)=n$, where $\sigma(k)$ is the sum of divisors of $k$. Is it true that $f(n)\leq n^{o(\frac{1}{\log\log n})}$? Perhaps even $\leq (\log n)^{O(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: This is discussed in problem B11 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1061
problems.statement_preview: How many solutions are there to $ \sigma(a)+\sigma(b)=\sigma(a+b) $ with $a+b\leq x$, where $\sigma$ is the sum of divisors function? Is it $\sim cx$ for some constant $c>0$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos reported in problem B15 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1062
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ be the size of the largest subset $A\subseteq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ such that there are no three distinct elements $a,b,c\in A$ such that $a\mid b$ and $a\mid c$. How large can $f(n)$ be? Is $\lim f(n)/n$ irrational?
problems.references_preview: The example $[m+1,3m+2]$ shows that $f(n)\geq\lceil \frac{2}{3}n\rceil$. Lebensold \cite{Le76} has shown that, for large $n$, $ 0.6725 n \leq f(n) \leq 0.6736 n. $ This is problem B24 in Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437. [Le76] Lebensold, Kenneth, A divisibility problem. Studies in Appl. Math…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1063
problems.statement_preview: Let $k\geq 2$ and define $n_k\geq 2k$ to be the least value of $n$ such that $n-i$ divides $\binom{n}{k}$ for all but one $0\leq i<k$. Estimate $n_k$.
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Selfridge posed in \cite{ErSe83}. Erdos and Selfridge noted (and a proof can be found in \cite{Mo85}) that if $n\geq 2k$ then there must exist at least one $0\leq i<k$ such that $n-i$ does not divide $\binom{n}{k}$. We have $n_2=4$, $n_3=6$, $n_4=9$, and $n_5=12$. Monier \cite{Mo85} observed that $n_k\leq k!$ for $k\geq 3$, since $\binom{k!}{k}$ is divis…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1065
problems.statement_preview: Are there infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p=2^kq+1$ for some prime $q$ and $k\geq 0$? Or $p=2^k3^lq+1$?
problems.references_preview: This is mentioned in problem B46 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}. References [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1066
problems.statement_preview: Let $G$ be a graph given by $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, where any two distinct points are at least distance $1$ apart, and we draw an edge between two points if they are distance $1$ apart. Let $g(n)$ be maximal such that any such graph always has an independent set on at least $g(n)$ vertices. Estimate $g(n)$, or perhaps $\lim \frac{g(n)}{n}$.
problems.references_preview: Such graphs are always planar. Erdos initially thought that $g(n)=n/3$, but Chung and Graham, and independently Pach, gave a construction that shows $g(n)\leq \frac{6}{19}n$. Pach and Toth \cite{PaTo96} improved this to $g(n)\leq \frac{5}{16}n$. Pollack \cite{Po85} noted that the Four colour theorem implies $g(n)\geq n/4$, since the graph is planar. Pollack reports that Pach o…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1068
problems.statement_preview: Does every graph with chromatic number $\aleph_1$ contain a countable subgraph which is infinitely vertex-connected?
problems.references_preview: I do not think this was originally a question of Erdos - it appears in \cite{BoPi24} as a 'version of the Erdos-Hajnal problem' (which is [1067]). I could not in fact find this in the paper of Erdos and Hajnal \cite{ErHa66}, however, and hence the first place it appears may in fact be in \cite{BoPi24}. In hindsight this should not have been included as a separate problem, but …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1070
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ be maximal such that, given any $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, there exist $f(n)$ points such that no two are distance $1$ apart. Estimate $f(n)$. In particular, is it true that $f(n)\geq n/4$?
problems.references_preview: In other words, estimate the minimal independence number of a unit distance graph with $n$ vertices. If $\omega$ is the independence number and $\chi$ is the chromatic number then $\omega \chi\geq n$, and hence $f(n)\geq n/\chi$, where $\chi$ is the answer to the Hadwiger-Nelson problem [508]. The Moser spindle shows $f(n)\leq \frac{2}{7}n\approx 0.285n$. Larman and Rogers \ci…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1071
problems.statement_preview: Is there a finite set of unit line segments (rotated and translated copies of $(0,1)$) in the unit square, no two of which intersect, which are maximal with respect to this property? Is there a region $R$ with a maximal set of disjoint unit line segments that is countably infinite?
problems.established_preview: Background states the first question has a positive answer: a finite maximal family of pairwise disjoint unit segments in the unit square exists.
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and T\'{o}th. The answer to the first question is yes (which Erdos gave Danzer \$10 for). There is no prize mentioned in \cite{Er87b} for the (still open) second question. There are two examples Erdos gives in \cite{Er87b}, the {IMAGE=1071-one,first} by Danzer, the {IMAGE=1071-two,second} by an unnamed participant. In \cite{Er87b} he further asks what happe…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1072
problems.statement_preview: For any prime $p$, let $f(p)$ be the least integer such that $f(p)!+1\equiv 0\pmod{p}$. Is it true that there are infinitely many $p$ for which $f(p)=p-1$? Is it true that $f(p)/p\to 0$ for almost all $p$?
problems.references_preview: Questions formulated by Erdos, Hardy, and Subbarao \cite{HaSu02}, who believed that the number of $p\leq x$ for which $f(p)=p-1$ is $o(x/\log x)$. These are mentioned in problem A2 of Guy's collection. References [HaSu02] Hardy, G. E. and Subbarao, M. V., A modified problem of Pillai and some related questions. Amer. Math. Monthly (2002), 554--559.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1073
problems.statement_preview: Let $A(x)$ count the number of composite $u<x$ such that $n!+1\equiv 0\pmod{u}$ for some $n$. Is it true that $A(x)\leq x^{o(1)}$?
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos raised in discussions with Hardy and Subbarao \cite{HaSu02}. The sequence of such $u$ begins $ 25,121,169,437,\ldots $ and is A256519 in the OEIS. Wilson's theorem states that $u$ is prime if and only if $(u-1)!+1\equiv 0\pmod{u}$. This is mentioned in problem A2 of Guy's collection. References [HaSu02] Hardy, G. E. and Subbarao, M. V., A modified problem o…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1074
problems.statement_preview: Let $S$ be the set of all $m\geq 1$ such that there exists a prime $p ot\equiv 1\pmod{m}$ such that $m!+1\equiv 0\pmod{p}$. Does $ \lim \frac{\lvert S\cap [1,x]\rvert}{x} $ exist? What is it? Similarly, if $P$ is the set of all primes $p$ such that there exists an $m$ with $p ot\equiv 1\pmod{m}$ such that $m!+1\equiv 0\pmod{p}$, then does $ \lim \frac{\lvert P\cap [1,x]\rvert}…
problems.references_preview: Questions raised by Erdos, Hardy, and Subbarao, who called the set $S$ 'EHS numbers' and the set $P$ 'Pillai primes', and proved that both $S$ and $P$ are infinite. Pillai \cite{Pi30} raised the question of whether there exist any primes in $P$. This was answered by Chowla, who noted that, for example, $ 14!+1\equiv 18!+1\equiv 0\pmod{23}. $ The sequence $S$ begins $ 8,9,13,14…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1075
problems.statement_preview: For `r>=3`, does edge count `>(1+eps)(n/r)^r` force a growing-si
problems.established_preview: Background gives Erdos' theorem with constant `c_r=r^{-r}` under a denser global hypothesis (at least `eps n^r` edges).
problems.references_preview: Erdos \cite{Er64f} proved that this is true with $c_r=r^{-r}$ whenever the graph has at least $\epsilon n^r$ many edges. References [Er64f] Erdos, P., On extremal problems of graphs and generalized graphs. Israel J. Math. (1964), 183--190.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1083
problems.statement_preview: Let $d\geq 3$, and let $f_d(n)$ be the minimal $m$ such that every set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$ determines at least $m$ distinct distances. Estimate $f_d(n)$ - in particular, is it true that $ f_d(n)=n^{\frac{2}{d}-o(1)}? $
problems.references_preview: A generalisation of the distinct distance problem [89] to higher dimensions. Erdos \cite{Er46b} proved $ n^{1/d}\ll_d f_d(n)\ll_d n^{2/d}, $ the upper bound construction being given by a set of lattice points. {UL} {LI} Clarkson, Edelsbrunner, Gubias, Sharir, and Welzl \cite{CEGSW90} proved $f_3(n)\gg n^{1/2}$.{/LI} {LI}Aronov, Pach, Sharir, and Tardos \cite{APST04} proved $f_…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1084
problems.statement_preview: Let $f_d(n)$ be minimal such that in any collection of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, all of distance at least $1$ apart, there are at most $f_d(n)$ many pairs of points which are distance $1$ apart. Estimate $f_d(n)$.
problems.references_preview: This is sometimes known as the contact number problem. It is easy to see that $f_1(n)=n-1$ and $f_2(n)<3n$ (since there can be at most $6$ points of distance $1$ from any point). Erdos \cite{Er46b} showed $ f_2(n)<3n-cn^{1/2} $ for some constant $c>0$, which the triangular lattice shows is the best possible up to the value of $c$. In \cite{Er75f} he speculated that the triangu…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1085
problems.statement_preview: Let $f_d(n)$ be minimal such that, in any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^d$, there exist at most $f_d(n)$ pairs of points which distance $1$ apart. Estimate $f_d(n)$.
problems.references_preview: The most difficult cases are $d=2$ and $d=3$. When $d=2$ this is the unit distance problem [90], and the best known bounds are $ n^{1+\frac{c}{\log\log n}}< f_2(n) \ll n^{4/3} $ for some constant $c>0$, the lower bound by Erdos \cite{Er46b} and the upper bound by Spencer, Szemer\'{e}di, and Trotter \cite{SST84}. When $d=3$ the best known bounds are $ n^{4/3}\log\log n \ll f_3(…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1086
problems.statement_preview: Let $g(n)$ be minimal such that any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ contains the vertices of at most $g(n)$ many triangles with the same area. Estimate $g(n)$.
problems.references_preview: Equivalently, how many triangles of area $1$ can a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine? Erdos and Purdy attribute this question to Oppenheim. Erdos and Purdy \cite{ErPu71} proved $ n^2\log\log n \ll g(n) \ll n^{5/2}, $ and believed the lower bound to be closer to the truth. The upper bound has been improved a number of times - by Pach and Sharir \cite{PaSh92}, Dumitr…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1087
problems.statement_preview: Estimate extremal number of "degenerate" 4-point subsets (with repeated pairwise distance) in `n`-point planar sets.
problems.references_preview: A question of Erdos and Purdy \cite{ErPu71}, who proved $ n^3\log n \ll f(n) \ll n^{7/2}. $ References [ErPu71] Erdos, Paul and Purdy, George, Some extremal problems in geometry. J. Combinatorial Theory Ser. A (1971), 246--252.", "difficulty": "L1" },{

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1088
problems.statement_preview: Estimate `f_d(n)`, the least `m` such that every `m` points in `R^d` contain `n` points with all pairwise distances distinct.
problems.references_preview: It is easy to prove that $f_d(n) \leq n^{O_d(1)}$. Erdos \cite{Er75f} claimed that he and Straus proved $f_d(n)\leq c_n^d$ for some constant $c_n>0$. When $d=1$ this is the subject of [530], and $f_1(n)\asymp n^2$. When $n=3$ this is the subject of [503]. Erdos could prove $f_2(3)=7$ and Croft \cite{Cr62} proved $f_3(3)=9$. The results described at [503] demonstrate that $f_d(…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1089
problems.statement_preview: Estimate `g_d(n)`, the least `m` such that every `m`-point set in `R^d` determines at least `n` distinct distances.
problems.references_preview: A question of Kelly. Erdos \cite{Er75f} writes it is 'easy' to see that $g_d(n)\gg d^{n-1}$. Erdos and Straus proved (in unpublished work mentioned in \cite{Er75f}) that $ g_d(n) \leq c^{d^{1-b_n}} $ for some constants $c>0$ and $b_n>0$. It is trivial that $g_1(3)=4$, and easy to see that $g_2(3)=6$. Croft \cite{Cr62} proved $g_3(3)=7$. The vertices of a $d$-dimensional cube d…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1091
problems.statement_preview: 1. If `G` is `K_4`-free with chromatic number `4`, must `G` contain an odd cycle with at least two diagonals? 2. More generally, is there `f(r)->infinity` such that every graph with chromatic number `4` and local condition `chi(H)<=3` for all `|V(H)|<=r` contains an odd cycle with at least `f(r)` diagonals?
problems.established_preview: Background states the first question was solved affirmatively by Voss (1982). So subquestion (1) is yes.
problems.references_preview: Erdos originally asked about the existence of just one diagonal, which is true, and was proved by Larson \cite{La79}. In fact Larson proved the following stronger conjecture of Bollob\'{a}s and Erdos: if $G$ is a $K_4$-free graph containing no odd cycle with a diagonal then either $G$ is bipartite, or $G$ contains a cut vertex, or $G$ contains a vertex with degree $\leq 2$. Th…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1092
problems.statement_preview: For the extremal function `f_r(n)` in the problem definition: 1. Is `f_2(n) >> n`? 2. More generally, is `f_r(n) >>_r n` for all `r`?
problems.established_preview: Background states that R"odl's construction (1982) disproves the first question. So `f_2(n) >> n` is false.
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Erdos, Hajnal, and Szemer\'{e}di. This seems to be closely related to, but distinct from, [744]. Tang notes in the comments that a construction of R"{o}dl \cite{Ro82} disproves the first question, so that $f_2(n) ot\gg n$. References [Ro82] R"{o}dl, Vojt\vEch, Nearly bipartite graphs with large chromatic number. Combinatorica (1982), 377-383.", "difficulty": "L…

Otherwise, the deficiency is the number of $0\leq i<k$ such that $n-i$ is $k$-smooth, that is, divisible only by primes $\leq k$. Are there infinitely many binomial coefficients with deficiency $1$? Are there only finitely …

In \cite{ELS93} they prove that if the deficiency exists and is $\geq 1$ then $n\ll 2^k\sqrt{k}$. The following examples are either from \cite{ELS88} or here. problems.title: Erdos Problem #1094
problems.statement_preview: For all $n\geq 2k$ the least prime factor of $\binom{n}{k}$ is $\leq \max(n/k,k)$, with only finitely many exceptions.
problems.references_preview: A stronger form of [384] that appears in a paper of Erdos, Lacampagne, and Selfridge \cite{ELS88}. Erdos observed that the least prime factor is always $\leq n/k$ provided $n$ is sufficiently large depending on $k$. Selfridge \cite{Se77} further conjectured that this always happens if $n\geq k^2-1$, except $\binom{62}{6}$. The threshold $g(k)$ below which $\binom{n}{k}$ is gua…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1095
problems.statement_preview: Let $g(k)>k+1$ be the smallest $n$ such that all prime factors of $\binom{n}{k}$ are $>k$. Estimate $g(k)$.
problems.references_preview: A question of Ecklund, Erdos, and Selfridge \cite{EES74}, who proved $ k^{1+c}<g(k)\leq \exp((1+o(1))k) $ for some constant $c>0$, and conjectured $g(k)<L_k=[1,\ldots,k]$, the least common multiple of all integers $\leq k$, for all large $k$. In \cite{EES74} they further conjecture that $ \limsup \frac{g(k+1)}{g(k)}=\infty $ and $ \liminf \frac{g(k+1)}{g(k)}=0. $ The lower bou…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1096
problems.statement_preview: Let $1<q<1+\epsilon$ and consider the set of numbers of the shape $\sum_{i\in S}q^i$ (for all finite $S$), ordered by size as $0=x_1<x_2<\cdots$. Is it true that, provided $\epsilon>0$ is sufficiently small, $x_{k+1}-x_k \to 0$?
problems.references_preview: A problem of Erdos and Jo\'{o} posed in the 1991 problem session of Great Western Number Theory. They speculate that the threshold may be $q_0$, where $q_0\approx 1.3247$ is the real root of $x^3=x+1$, and is the smallest Pisot-Vijayaraghavan number. In \cite{EJK90} Erd\H{o}, Jo\'{o}, and Komornik prove that any Pisot-Vijayaraghavan number cannot have this property, and also p…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1097
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be a set of $n$ integers. How many distinct $d$ can occur as the common difference of a three-term arithmetic progression in $A$? Are there always $O(n^{3/2})$ many such $d$?
problems.references_preview: A problem Erdos posed in the 1989 problem session of Great Western Number Theory. He states that Erdos and Ruzsa gave an explicit construction which achieved $n^{1+c}$ for some $c>0$, and Erdos and Spencer gave a probabilistic proof which achieved $n^{3/2}$, and speculated this may be the best possible. In the comment section, Chan has noticed that this problem is exactly equi…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1100
problems.statement_preview: If $1=d_1<\cdots<d_{\tau(n)}=n$ are the divisors of $n$, then let $\tau_\perp(n)$ count the number of $i$ for which $(d_i,d_{i+1})=1$. Is it true that $\tau_\perp(n)/\omega(n)\to \infty$ for almost all $n$? Is it true that $ \tau_\perp(n)< \exp((\log n)^{o(1)}) $ for all $n$? Let $ g(k) = \max_{\omega(n)=k}\tau_\perp(n), $ where $\omega(n)$ counts the number of distinct prime …

Erdos and Hall prove, for all $\epsilon>0$ and sufficiently large $x$, $ \max_{n<x} \tau_\perp(n) > \exp((\log\log x)^{2-\epsilon}). $ Erdos and Simonovits (see \cite{Er81h}) proved $ (2^{1/2}+o(1))^k < g(k) < (2…

For any $x$ define $t_x$ by $ u_1\cdots u_{t_x}\leq x< u_1\cdots u_{t_x}u_{t_x+1}. $ We call such a sequence $u_i$ good if, for all $\epsilon>0$, if $x…

He proved the existence of some good sequence (in which all the $u_i$ are primes). problems.title: Erdos Problem #1103
problems.statement_preview: Let $A$ be an infinite sequence of integers such that every $n\in A+A$ is squarefree. How fast must $A$ grow?
problems.references_preview: Erdos notes there exists such a sequence which grows exponentially, but does not expect such a sequence of polynomial growth. In \cite{Er81h} he asked whether there is an infinite sequence of integers $A$ such that, for every $a\in A$ and prime $p$, if $ a\equiv t\pmod{p^2} $ then $1\leq t<p^2/2$. He noted that such a sequence has the property that every $n\in A+A$ is squarefr…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1104
problems.statement_preview: Let $f(n)$ be the maximum possible chromatic number of a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices. Estimate $f(n)$.
problems.references_preview: The best bounds available are $ (1-o(1))(n/\log n)^{1/2}\leq f(n) \leq (2+o(1))(n/\log n)^{1/2}. $ The upper bound is due to Davies and Illingworth \cite{DaIl22}, the lower bound follows from a construction of Hefty, Horn, King, and Pfender \cite{HHKP25}. One can ask a similar question for the maximum possible chromatic number of a triangle-free graph on $m$ edges. Let this be…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1105
problems.statement_preview: The anti-Ramsey number $\mathrm{AR}(n,G)$ is the maximum possible number of colours in which the edges of $K_n$ can be coloured without creating a rainbow copy of $G$ (i.e. one in which all edges have different colours). Let $C_k$ be the cycle on $k$ vertices. Is it true that $ \mathrm{AR}(n,C_k)=\left(\frac{k-2}{2}+\frac{1}{k-1}\right)n+O(1)? $ Let $P_k$ be the path on $k$ ve…
problems.references_preview: A conjecture of Erdos, Simonovits, and S\'{o}s \cite{ESS75}, who gave a simple proof that $\mathrm{AR}(n,C_3)=n-1$. In this paper they announced proofs of the claimed formula for $\mathrm{AR}(n,P_k)$ for $n\geq \frac{5}{4}k+C$ for some large constant $C$, and also for all $n\geq k$ if $k$ is sufficiently large, but these never appeared. Simonovits and S\'{o}s \cite{SiSo84} pub…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1106
problems.statement_preview: Let $p(n)$ denote the partition function of $n$ and let $F(n)$ count the number of distinct prime factors of $ \prod_{1\leq k\leq n}p(k). $ Does $F(n)\to \infty$ with $n$? Is $F(n)>n$ for all sufficiently large $n$?
problems.references_preview: Asked by Erdos at Oberwolfach in 1986. Schinzel noted in the Oberwolfach problem book that $F(n)\to \infty$ follows from the asymptotic formula for $p(n)$ and a result of Tijdeman \cite{Ti73}. This is not obvious; details are given in a paper of Erdos and Ivi\'{c} (see page 69 of \cite{ErIv90}). Schinzel and Wirsing \cite{ScWi87} have proved $F(n) \gg \log n$. Ono \cite{On00} …

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1107
problems.statement_preview: Let $r\geq 2$. A number $n$ is $r$-powerful if for every prime $p$ which divides $n$ we have $p^r\mid n$. Is every large integer the sum of at most $r+1$ many $r$-powerful numbers?
problems.references_preview: Given in the 1986 Oberwolfach problem book as a problem of Erdos and Ivi\'{c}. This is true when $r=2$, as proved by Heath-Brown \cite{He88} (see [941]). See [940] for the problem of which integers are the sum of at most $r$ many $r$-powerful numbers. References [He88] Heath-Brown, D. R., Ternary quadratic forms and sums of three square-full numbers. (1988), 137--163.", "diffi…

problems.title: Erdos Problem #1108
problems.statement_preview: Let $ A = \left\{ \sum_{n\in S}n! : S\subset \mathbb{N}\textrm{ finite}\right\}. $ If $k\geq 2$, then does $A$ contain only finitely many $k$th powers? Does it contain only finitely many powerful numbers?
